The approach that I saw in that branch looks good to me. I'd recommend leaving `dict` as the base class, unless you have something specific driving the choice to make it something else.

In functional programming you'll tend to really good uses of data types. I think that `InlineTableDict` as a sentinel subclass is a good example of that. I think that I'd use something similar if I were writing this in Haskell, for example.

If this is _not_ a public interface, then consumers of this library are not able to write a _new_ toml file, without reading an old one, and have it use any inline tables. I think that there should be a way for users that don't need `load` or `loads` at all to still be able to make inline tables with `dump` and `dumps`. From there, it seems natural to expose `InlineTableDict` as the way for users to do it.

I like the functional approach, so the Encoder/Decoder classes don't jive well with my own thoughts. However, I've not used the json equivalents except in the most trivial of ways (just copy-pasting, basically), so I may not be a good judge of whether they are indeed the better approach.

I think at this point this particular PR is dead, and your work on that other branch should be how we implement what this desires, so I'm gonna go ahead and close it.

All values are coerced after substitutions. Also, this doesn't ever use the base ContentHandler, which might actually cause problems being `identity`. It could, instead, simply `pass` instead of returning a value.

The error means that the compiler finds a `gl.h` include before the `glad.h` include, which can be through any dependency, most likely GLFW.

A simple include graph:
![graph](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/255721/26033329/40582aee-38aa-11e7-8939-bf3506fa3e13.png)

Your `main.cpp` includes `core/game_superclass.h` which then includes `core/poll_keyboard.h` very early, which has a dependency on glfw, which then includes `gl.h`, later on you will include glad, but by this time `gl.h` was already included (Fuck yeah C!).

I defined `GLFW_INCLUDE_NONE` in your `main.cpp` (it makes glfw not include `g.h` publically), but then compilation fails because of missing types in `core/camera_data.h`.

Probably easiest fix is including glad at the very top of `core/game_superclass.h`, imo a better way of handling this include mess is creating a header file which includes gl for you and one which includes glfw for you, in the first you include second in the latter you either define the `GLFW_INCLUDE_NONE` or include your gl header before glfw. And whenever you need gl or glfw you include either of these headers.

Minimap2's "ts" is different from gmap's "XS". For gmap, "XS" is the strand of the transcript (inferred from the read) relative to the reference genome. For minimap2, "ts" is the strand of the read relative to the transcript. When a read is mapped to the reverse strand of the genome, "ts" is the opposite of "XS".

In your example, the read is mapped to reverse strand of the genome. Gene UBC at chr12: 124911646 is on the reverse strand of the genome, too. This means the read is on the same strand as UBC transcripts, so you see "ts:A:+".

If you want to get gmap's XS, you need to flip minimap2's ts if the read is mapped to reverse strand of the genome.

I modified `low_level_mouse_handler` callback  in `_winmouse.py` to calculate time difference between previous and current event and create new `ButtonEvent` of type `DOUBLE` if the difference is less or equal to the value returned by `GetDoubleClickTime`.

```
    GetDoubleClickTime = user32.GetDoubleClickTime

    ...

    previous_button_event = None # defined in global scope

    ...

    def low_level_mouse_handler(nCode, wParam, lParam):
        global previous_button_event

        struct = lParam.contents
        # Can't use struct.time because it's usually zero.
        t = time.time()

        if wParam == WM_MOUSEMOVE:
            event = MoveEvent(struct.x, struct.y, t)
        elif wParam == WM_MOUSEWHEEL:
            event = WheelEvent(struct.data / (WHEEL_DELTA * (2<<15)), t)
        elif wParam in buttons_by_wm_code:
            type, button = buttons_by_wm_code.get(wParam, ('?', '?'))
            if wParam >= WM_XBUTTONDOWN:
                button = {0x10000: X, 0x20000: X2}[struct.data]
            event = ButtonEvent(type, button, t)

            if (event.event_type == DOWN) and previous_button_event is not None:
                diff = int(round(event.time * 1000.0)) - int(round(previous_button_event.time * 1000.0))
                if diff <= GetDoubleClickTime():
                    event = ButtonEvent(DOUBLE, event.button, event.time)

            previous_button_event = event

        queue.put(event)
        return CallNextHookEx(NULL, nCode, wParam, lParam)
```
So far this works as intented and generates `UP, DOWN, DOUBLE, UP` event sequence which is a valid double click event sequence according to this [MSDN article](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/gg153548(v=vs.85).aspx).

What do you think of this solution?

I didn't read the C code, but I believe there's a simple way to deal with it: `distutils.extension.Extention` has an `optional` argument, which ignores a failed extension build if `optional=True`. So, this works (alongside with the `print` updates, of course):

    ext_modules = [ Extension('srp._srp', ['srp/_srp.c',], libraries = ['ssl',], optional=True) ]

Also, minor nitpick: I've read this patch's updates to print in `test_srp.py` and I don't think they're quite right. You should be using `from __future__ import print_function`, as print has a different behavior in Py2 or Py3. For example `print '       |',` _does not_ translate to `print('       |',)`, but rather `print('       |', end=' ')`.

After talking with @jonchar, in order for the API design to be of the form:

```
c = CircosPlot(graph=G, node_order='kw1', node_size='kw2', node_grouping='kw3', node_colour='kw4', edge_width='kw5', edge_colour='kw6', data_properties=some_dictionary)
```

the data has to be checked first to make sure that the data keyed by the node and edge metadata keywords (`kwX`) fit the type of data that can be plotted. Probably best to implement the data checking functionality on the `BasePlot()` object so that it's available for all children instantiated. These data checks should also be stored as an attribute, so that they can be passed to other plotting objects if needed.

The structure of the `plot.data_properties` attribute could be a dictionary of dictionaries:

```
{node_kw:{kw1:'categorical', kw2:'quantitative', kw3:'ordinal',... },...}
```

Providing the `data_properties` keyword argument will allow the end user to provide this up-front, thus allowing us to bypass the checks, and raise loud errors if they don't turn out to match.

>>> type(zenpy_client.views.tickets(view_id))

    print(ticket)

The `eb` used there is defined thus:

```
def eb(query):
    """
    Escape characters in a query according to RFC4515
    ‘*’ -> ‘\\2A’, ‘(‘ -> ‘\\28’, ‘)’ -> ‘\\29’, ‘\’ -> ‘\\5C’, chr(0) -> ‘\\00’
    """
    return (
        query
            .replace('\\', '\\5C')
            .replace('(', '\\28')
            .replace(')', '\\29')
            .replace('*', '\\2A')
            .replace('\x00', '\\00')
    )
```

Normally the `/arch:SSE2` switch needs to be given to visual studio to use SSE2 instructions.  When it is those preprocessor statements check the `_M_IX86_FP` macro which is set by `/arch:SSE2` and will automatically turn on SSE2 use in dlib.

I checked shared_ptr_thread_safe usage and it looks like it is involved in the threading part of dlib. Are you sure you want to replace them blindly? The point is, I see two solutions to this problem; the first one puts the build complexity into upstream's build system (CMake scripts generating both shared and static files "at once"), and the second puts it into the packaging system (builds two times with two different configurations).

I usually tend to avoid placing too much complexity into the packaging system because my conception of a package is that it should be as straightforward as possible. However in this case, maintaining an alternative CMake config is going to be really painful, while writing the packaging system's rules so that it builds two times won't.

So, I guess the solution is pretty evident.

Yep @agonen, it ended up being many orders of magnitude faster to do it the way @jruizgit suggested. The reason is that each time it would be joining on attributes that shared a common object id (`nmid` in my example), it had to test every micro-fact against each other, in effect doing a cartesian join (I'm admittedly making some assumptions here, and have not strictly verified the mechanics of this on the backend). 

So, suppose I have 5 objects with 10 attributes and want to find a fact having `item == 'txn'` and `status == 'pending'`. 
* If each of the 5 objects is stored as a single fact with 10 attributes, then all the rule engine has to do is scan across those 5 objects and look at those two attributes (5x2 = 10 comparisons total). If those 5 objects are stored as 50 micro-facts linked by a `obj_id` attribute (eg, `{'obj_id':1,'item':'txn'}` etc), then to match `item` and `status` together through `obj_id`, the rule engine would be checking across **1225 combinations** (`C(50,2)`).
* Now suppose we add a third condition such that `value > 500`. In this case, you'd be looking at `C(50,3)` = **39200 combinations**. 
* Adding a fourth parameter into the condition bumps it up to almost **half a million comparisons** to make. So you can see it quickly grows out of control especially as conditions become increasingly complex. Meanwhile, for that same scenario where we have a condition that looks at 4 parameters, if we have only 5 objects with 10 attributes each, the engine only needs to make 20 comparisons. 
* (_Admittedly, this is a dumbed down worst-case scenario and I'm pretty sure durable's execution is more optimized than this, but it should at least give a sense of how things scale for the two methods._) 

Anyway, on an empirical level, using micro-facts caused the rule execution to come to a veritable crawl once I got a couple dozen micro-facts and a handful of mildly complex rules (involving maybe 4-8 attributes each on average) together. On the flip side, switching over to the retract & assert method of modifying facts is very efficient and with those same facts and rules (and even more, as my rule base has grown), rules are executing at <0.05s a piece.

I agree this is a bug and needs a fix.
However note that your example should actually fail in strict PEP 484 sense, because ``a: Dict`` should be treated like ``a: Dict[Any, Any]`` and the type parameters of ``Dict`` are invariant. That said, pytypes is intentionally less strict (by default) than PEP 484 because I found this behaviour too counter-intuitive. ``pytypes.covariant_mapping = False`` would cause strict PEP 484 behavior (default is ``True``).

Also note that pytypes has a flag to treat undeclared type parameters as unknown rather than ``Any``. (``pytypes.strict_unknown_check``). One can also prohibit missing type parameters at all by setting ``pytypes.check_unbound_types = False``, which lets pytypes throw an error as soon as an incompletely declared type is encountered (intended for finding holes in your one's type annotations).

As you can see I am actually doing the TZERO myself, cfitsio is not doing it.

There is no natural way to do deal with this given that the column is in fact declared as signed 8 byte.  This is a giant kludge propagated in the cfitsio code base.  It can work there because the user can always case the type as needed.

However fitsio always assumes that you want to read into the type that is declared in the file; how else can we sensibly interpret the file without user override?  Also fitsio internally reserves the right to read the bytes directly for big efficiency gains (hence doing tzero myself), so remapping the type won't work.

A dumb kludge would be for the user to tell fitsio to override the data type, but this would require twice the memory, since we would have to make a new version of the data and copy into it.

However using `use_2to3` does not help, when using `py.test`, since pytest keeps importing the local code instead of the py3 version, and then throws the "unicode is not defined" error.

There is a [blog post](https://www.hackzine.org/python-testing-with-pytest-and-2to3-plus-tox-and-travis-ci.html) which explains how to integrate distutils using 2to3 and pytest.

 You would want the disconnect() at the very end of your script (i.e. outside the for-loop).

Okay, I thought about this further, also in the context of MAM.

I think that we should strip the Conversation interface a little, so that it only does the following:

* Provide Member objects representing the members of the conversation
* Handle member management (kick, ban, invite, …), + events
* Associate inbound messages with Members and provide a callback which emits the message plus the associated Member
* Send messages to the conversation, possibly with its own tracking implementation.

Everything beyond that, like interpreting Chat States, Chat Markers, Read Reciepts etc. should be left to the application, with one caveat: Things which implement Message Tracking parts (e.g. Read Reciepts) of course can use the normal tracking API (which is based on stanza filters anyways).

The idea behind this approach is that the same message pipeline which is used to interpret Chat States etc. in a live conversation can be used by the application to interpret these things when querying MAM. It will be up to the application how much and which state is persisted.

While we could provide a very generic implementation in aioxmpp, I think this is territory which should be explored in jclib first.

This also saves us from the "firewall" issue (determine where to send chat states automatically).

For Chat States specifically, I think an object which can be attached to a Conversation (we have the early callback I spoke about earlier now) makes most sense. It would hook into the on_message event to interpret inbound Chat States (without filtering them out) and provide a method to map a Member to a Chat State. It should also have an event signalling a Chat State change and a method to feed stanzas and set chat states manually, e.g. for history replay.

Does that make sense?

@zspitz The subfolder does not have to be ```lib\site-packages``` it could be any place which is defined set to the variable ```win32com.__gen_path__```. 
The ProgramData folder is by default also only writeable for administrators and not users, since the location is shared between users and one user should not influence other users. The best place to write files for a user is ```FOLDERID_LocalAppData``` (see https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/dd378457(v=vs.85).aspx) since it is writeable and not roaming (a com cache makes only sense on the PC where the program is installed). 
If you want to share cache files between users the default place would be ```FOLDERID_PublicDocuments```.

If you could provide a more complete example I would really appreciate it, someone else had mentioned some issues using the `logging` module and I would like to see what's going on. I was actually able to get some logging working with the following:

```python
from deco import *
import logging

logging.basicConfig()
logger = logging.getLogger()

@concurrent
def my_conc(i):
    logger.warning(i)


@synchronized
def sync():
    for i in range(10):
        my_conc(i)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sync()
```
This actually does print stuff out, but I suspect your problem is more nuanced than this simple example. There are two things that I think could be going on with your program.

**Logging Thread Safety vs. Process Safety**
The logging module only claims that it is thread safe, not process safe. This could mean simply that some of your log lines might get interspersed, or something worse like the logging module's state becomes inconsistent.

**Module Globals in Multiprocessing**
When the multiprocessing module spawns new processes, module globals get recreated. The problem is that any changes that happen to the module globals in the parent process aren't necessarily reflected in the child process. At one point deco had a solution to this, but it turned out to contain some bugs and ultimately was discarded because of its complexity. I would be very interested in adding this back in if it fixes problems like these.

The easiest way to fix these is just use `@concurrent.threaded` instead of `@concurrent` so `deco` will use threads instead of processes. The obvious downside is if your concurrent function is compute bound, you won't see any performance increase. If it's IO bound like waiting on network resources etc, this would be a good choice.

**TL;DR:**
I'd be interested to see any example code you could provide, it could prove very useful.
In the near term you might consider using `@concurrent.threaded` instead of `@concurrent` to try to fix the issue with `logging`.

The request headers contain `Cache-Control:no-cache` and also don't specify `If-Modified-Since`. This would imply the cache is disabled, which it is by default when using the developer tools. Make sure to uncheck the `disable cache` check-box on the Chrome devtools "Network" tab. Chrome and Firefox also skip the HTTP 304 in many cases (in Chrome's case due to recently added logic, for Firefox due to WhiteNoise using the `immutable` directive) and show a fake HTTP 200 from memory cache (but says zero bytes transferred).

Also when refreshing the page do a standard refresh and not a "shift+refresh".

To demonstrate this is working:
```
$ curl -sSfI http://trip2go.tours/static/site/js/t2g-polyfills.615dcc41d2ff.js | grep 'Last-Modified'
Last-Modified: Thu, 13 Apr 2017 08:53:46 GMT

$ curl -I http://trip2go.tours/static/site/js/t2g-polyfills.615dcc41d2ff.js -H 'If-Modified-Since: Thu, 13 Apr 2017 08:53:46 GMT'
HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified
Server: nginx/1.10.0 (Ubuntu)
Date: Thu, 13 Apr 2017 09:48:04 GMT
Content-Length: 0
Connection: keep-alive
X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN
Vary: Accept-Encoding, Accept-Language
Content-Language: en
Cache-Control: max-age=315360000, public, immutable
```

The comments aren't being used to track any state about the post, only to provide instructions to the user. I took the idea from git, for example when you make a new commit in git it opens a temporary file in your editor with the following text:

```
# Please enter the commit message for your changes. Lines starting
# with '#' will be ignored, and an empty message aborts the commit.
#
# On branch master
# Your branch is up to date with 'origin/master'.
#
# Changes to be committed:
#       new file:   tmp.txt
```

---

To be clear, this is what you're suggesting w/ number 4?

```
<!--f8das67dSAH&8h
Please type your submission below this comment block.

The first line will be interpreted as the title.
The following lines will be interpreted ad the content.

Posting to /r/WritingPrompts
-->
```

Overall I think this works out pretty well. It's still not as straightforward as using ``#``, but it's probably worth the tradeoff for fully supporting markdown. There's a small chance that if the user isn't familiar with HTML they might screw it up by typing their post inside of the closing tag, or inadvertently deleting the closing tag. Since the random string is only needed to prevent collisions, it doesn't need to be unique and could be hardcoded in the RTV configuration or even set to something more legible like this:

```
<!--RTV+INSTRUCTIONS
Please type your submission below this comment block.

The first line will be interpreted as the title.
The following lines will be interpreted as the content.

Posting to /r/WritingPrompts
-->
```

Hi, thanks for the PR! I looked over it briefly and I think most of the clean-up code looks good ;) I would say don't go crazy on refactoring but if you notice simple things like the configuration loading changes you made, those are fair game. 

It's still a small project so the issue tracker is your best bet for communication. I prefer that to email because it leaves a public record and other have a chance to join in the conversation. Feel free to post general discussion or questions there too, it doesn't have to be limited to specific issues.

A couple comments on the KeyboardInterruptible
- First off, I really like the idea of cancelling loads and I think it's a valuable feature to add.
- Why are you altering SIGINT? I'm not too familiar with it but from what I understand  (http://stackoverflow.com/a/1112350) if you send a signal to kill the program, it should kill the program. If I type ``kill rtv`` the whole thing should exit immediately no matter what is happening inside.
- Would it make sense to add this functionality directly into the LoadScreen class? That would theoretically cover every time we could want to cancel something from loading.
- Continuing on the previous points, to me it feels more intuitive to use the ``esc`` key to cancel. Escape is already used in other places in the code to cancel operations, and there's an EscapeInterrupt exception that you can raise. Would be a bit harder to implement (maybe add a stdscr.getch() to the loading-screen's background thread that raises an exception in when esc is pressed?). It just feels dirty to override ctrl-c to mean something other than "terminate".

There was some work a while ago to implement this https://github.com/michael-lazar/rtv/pull/97, but it has since been abandoned. Certainly having support for custom themes would be a good thing to have. 

For now, I agree that changing your terminal's colorscheme is the easiest way to do it. RTV only uses the first 8 colors (black, white, red, green, blue, cyan, blue, magenta).

Thank you for creating an issue, it sounds like a reasonable thing to support. So if I understand correctly (from https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/4861), RTV should technically be checking for ``$VISUAL`` first and then falling back to ``$EDITOR`` if it isn't defined? So the new try order would be:

1. ``$RTV_EDITOR``
2. ``$VISUAL``
3. ``$EDITOR``
4. ``nano``

You're welcome to make a PR, I agree with you that this should be a very simple feature to add.

Attach the steps here for those who is interested in this:
```
~: export PATH=$PATH:<PATH_TO_DIFF_SCRIPT_DIR>
~: git config --global diff.tool <DIFF_SCRIPT_NAME>
~: git config --global difftool.<DIFF_SCRIPT_NAME>.cmd '<DIFF_SCRIPT_NAME>  $REMOTE $LOCAL $MERGED'
#$REMOTE $LOCAL $MERGED are variables passed to the <DIFF_SCRIPT> by git.
```
after the steps above, we can use `git difftool` instead of `git diff` to show the differences with deepdiff, all the arguments are the same

1. I've updated the converters (below), including a ~45-fold speedup for binary data. The logging calls presuppose a Python logger called 'logger', and some inputs are also presupposed.
2. When accessing databases having VARBINARY(MAX) fields, via the Microsoft SQL Server JDBC Driver 4.1 (via jpype via jaydebeapi), data-fetching functions crash when they exit (as the database cursor they own goes out of scope and is deleted). Internally, as determined by attaching the gdb debugger, the crash is at a recv() call, itself called by Java.java.net.SocketInputStream.socketRead0(). This one took me a while to understand and work around! This crash can be averted by passing the "responseBuffering=adaptive" setting when opening the JDBC connection, e.g. via the connection URL; see https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms378988(SQL.90).aspx .

The project is an anonymisation tool, by the way: https://github.com/RudolfCardinal/anonymise

Converters:

```
def _convert_java_binary(rs, col):
    # https://github.com/originell/jpype/issues/71
    # http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5088671
    # https://github.com/baztian/jaydebeapi/blob/master/jaydebeapi/__init__.py
    # https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms378813(v=sql.110).aspx
    # http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2920364/checking-for-a-null-int-value-from-a-java-resultset  # noqa

    v = None
    logger.debug("_convert_java_binary: converting...")
    time1 = time.time()
    try:
        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # Method 1: 3578880 bytes in 21.7430660725 seconds =   165 kB/s
        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # java_val = rs.getObject(col)
        # if java_val is None:
        #     return
        # t = str(type(java_val))
        # if t == "<class 'jpype._jarray.byte[]'>": ...
        # v = ''.join(map(lambda x: chr(x % 256), java_val))

        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # Method 2: 3578880 bytes in 8.07930088043 seconds =   442 kB/s
        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # java_val = rs.getObject(col)
        # if java_val is None:
        #     return
        # l = len(java_val)
        # v = bytearray(l)
        # for i in xrange(l):
        #     v[i] = java_val[i] % 256

        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # Method 3: 3578880 bytes in 20.1435189247 seconds =   177 kB/s
        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # java_val = rs.getObject(col)
        # if java_val is None:
        #     return
        # v = bytearray(map(lambda x: x % 256, java_val))

        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        # Method 4: 3578880 bytes in 0.48352599144 seconds = 7,402 kB/s
        # ---------------------------------------------------------------------
        j_hexstr = rs.getString(col)
        if rs.wasNull():
            return
        v = binascii.unhexlify(j_hexstr)

    finally:
        time2 = time.time()
        logger.debug("... done (in {} seconds)".format(time2 - time1))
        return v


def _convert_java_bigstring(rs, col):
    v = str(rs.getCharacterStream(col))
    if rs.wasNull():
        return None
    return v


def _convert_java_bigint(rs, col):
    # http://stackoverflow.com/questions/26899595
    # https://github.com/baztian/jaydebeapi/issues/6
    # https://github.com/baztian/jaydebeapi/blob/master/jaydebeapi/__init__.py
    # https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/math/BigInteger.html
    # http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/sql/ResultSet.html
    java_val = rs.getObject(col)
    if java_val is None:
        return
    v = getattr(java_val, 'toString')()  # Java call: java_val.toString()
    return int(v)


def reconfigure_jaydebeapi():
    from jaydebeapi.dbapi2 import _DEFAULT_CONVERTERS
    _DEFAULT_CONVERTERS.update({
        'BIGINT': _convert_java_bigint,

        'BINARY': _convert_java_binary,  # overrides an existing one
        'BLOB': _convert_java_binary,
        'LONGVARBINARY': _convert_java_binary,
        'VARBINARY': _convert_java_binary,

        'LONGVARCHAR': _convert_java_bigstring,
        'LONGNVARCHAR': _convert_java_bigstring,
    })


reconfigure_jaydebeapi()
```

Curious to see if there was a reasonably straightforward explanation for the discrepancies between the UTF-8 bytes and the resulting Unicode characters in the above examples, I did a bit of digging. In the following descriptions,  `W+` refers to Windows-1252 characters and `U+` refers to Unicode characters.

#### First example

The Maple Leaf character `U+1F341` is UTF-8 encoded as `F0 9F 8D 81`. Characters in query: `U+F0, U+178, U+8D, and U+81`.

```
W+F0 -> Latin Small Letter Eth -> U+F0
W+9F -> Latin Capital Letter Y With Diaeresis -> U+178
W+8D -> (unused code point) -> U+8D
W+81 -> (unused code point) -> U+81
```

#### Second example

Ώπα is UTF-8 encoded as `CE 8F CF 80 CE B1`. Characters in query: `U+CE U+8F U+CF U+AC U+CE U+B1`.

```
W+CE -> Latin Capital Letter I With Circumflex -> U+CE
W+8F -> (unused code point) -> U+8F
W+CF -> Latin Capital Letter I With Diaeresis -> U+CF
W+80 -> Euro Sign -> U+20AC -> (apparently truncated to) U+AC
W+CE -> Latin Capital Letter I With Circumflex -> U+CE
W+B1 -> Plus-Minus Sign -> U+B1
```

eppy is dependent on reading and parsing IDD file. As long as the IDD file is read correctly, it should largely work in terms of reading the objects and fields and editing them.

One way to test this is to 
- read the idf file with eppy and write it back to disk (without comments)
- now read the idf file with a simple python script and write it disk without comments (use a identical format to eppy output)
- if the two files match, the test passes
- Do this for all the example files in E+
- If all of them pass, we are good.

Cool. I've been thinking about what might be the best API.

Currently the PR lets you set the codec on a per-server basis, as well as per-channel for the client-side.

I already noticed a desire on my part to have a single server, with multiple APIs using different codecs, i.e.

```python
server = Server(
    [existing_protocol_buffers_api, new_messagepack_api],
)
```

I do not like how that would require a change in how simple and easy the interface and implementation of the `Server` class is, i.e. this:

```python
server = Server()
server.add(existing_protocol_buffers_api)
server.add(new_messagepack_api, codec=MessagePack)
```

strikes me as suboptimal.

We could move it one step down and allow each handler to specify a codec. This would also solve my problem, because I could do:

```python
class NewMessagePackAPI:
    def __mapping__(self):
        return {
            '/Foo/Bar': grpclib.const.Handler(
                self.foobar,
                grpclib.const.Cardinality.UNARY_STREAM,
                codec=MessagePack
            )
        }

new_messagepack_api = NewMessagePackAPI()
server = Server(
    [existing_protocol_buffers_api, new_messagepack_api],
)
```

The elephant in the room of course is the content type. Codecs could be registered for particular content types, which seems like the way to go, except that we are not really talking about a dump wire format here.

- In the case of a MessagePack codec, the developer is sending certain objects in and expecting certain objects out. We can't let the other side determine the content type, and suddenly code that was requiring a protobuf object is given a Python dict.

- In the case of pyrobuf, the content type would remain the same, and should also not be selectable by the peer. And rather than setting it on a per-handler basis, we'd likely want to switch it out globally.

There are some places in the code where if a message is received while `stop_running_event` has been set, it will not be written.

1. At the start, if `stop_running_event` is already set, database will be closed without writing any messages in the queue.
2. At the second `get_message`, if this is received after `MAX_TIME_BETWEEN_WRITES` has passed, it will not be added to `messages`, instead `m` will be garbage collected.
3. When writing to the database, any new messages during that time will not be checked.

My idea was to also write the rest of the messages in the queue before closing the database.

The Flesch Reading Score resulting in a negative score is perfectly valid, and the highest score is 121.22.

> The highest (easiest) readability score possible is 121.22, (i.e. every sentence consisting of only one one-syllable words).

> While Amazon calculates the text of Moby Dick as 57.9, one particularly long sentence about sharks in chapter 64 has a readability score of −146.77.

Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flesch%E2%80%93Kincaid_readability_tests#Flesch_reading_ease)

I will make a PR to add a note about this in the README.

```python
from textstat.textstat import textstat

textstat.flesch_reading_ease("cat")

textstat.flesch_reading_ease("""

The multiplication just uses numpy.dot underneath, so the multiplication should be correct.

I think what's happened is the ordering in the object is logically backwards.
Because matrix*.multiply is a proxy to numpy.dot, you normally would do `mv = np.dot(model_view, projection)`.
As seen in this old code in PyGLy
```
self._matrix = matrix44.multiply(
                    self._transform.matrix,
                    self.parent.matrix
                    )
```

This seems to have been forgotten and the objects are multiplying as `multiply(parent, self)` rather than `multiply(self, parent)`.
You proved this by reversing the order of your multiplications.

You need to follow these steps

1. On your [codacy dashboard](https://www.codacy.com/app/danielhrisca/asammdf/dashboard)
2. Select `Setup Coverage`
3. Copy `codacy token` xxxxx
4. Go to [travis-ci](https://travis-ci.org/danielhrisca/asammdf)
5. Go to `More Options` -> `Settings`
6. Add new environment variable `CODACY_PROJECT_TOKEN` and paste the `codacy token` 
    - make sure `Display value in build log` is set to `Off`

7. Update markdown badge to `README.md`
    - Take the coverage badge from your [codacy Settings](https://www.codacy.com/app/yahym/github_publish/settings)
    - Make a push with new `README.md` to trigger a build
8. Done. You should have the coverage added as a badge from your project (which makes more sense than from mine :)).

I had a look into this issue. It seems to be caused by identical unnamed expressions in the grammar (such as item* in the above test case).  When the references are resolved, the first occurence of the expression is added to the 'done' set, and then when the second occurrence is resolved it compares equal to the first because they are the same class and have the same members. I was able to work around the issue by defining named expressions for problematic subexpressions.  There must be some further subtleties as it only seems to happen for some cases, I think something to do with the fact that the expression's members are only rewritten once all the members have been processed.

So that for now in my Flask-Restful appliction I have to use dirty workaround like listed below:

from flask import current_app
from flask_jwt import current_user
from flask_restful import Resource


def generate_token(user):
    """ Currently this is workaround
    since the latest version that already has this function
    is not published on PyPI yet and we don't want
    to install the package directly from GitHub.
    See: https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-jwt/blob/9f4f3bc8dce9da5dd8a567dfada0854e0cf656ae/flask_jwt/__init__.py#L145
    """
    jwt = current_app.extensions['jwt']
    payload = jwt.payload_callback(user)
    token = jwt.encode_callback(payload)
    return token


class BaseResource(Resource):
    def dispatch_request(self, *args, **kwargs):
        response = super(Resource, self).dispatch_request(*args, **kwargs)
        if current_user:
            response[2]['Fresh-Token'] = generate_token(current_user)
        return response
```

My 2 cents: When generating reports, it's sometimes useful to leave an explicit record of what has been exercised by the tests, and not only the errors. We have a usecase where we want to compare generated data against ground truth values. Since we don't necessarily compare all reported data, it's convenient to see at a glance what things are being compared, even when the comparison is successful.

The [HTMLTestRunner](https://github.com/tungwaiyip/HTMLTestRunner) package present results in a compact format that doesn't impose a lot of clutter on the eye:
* Single HTML report for all testcases. Currently tracked in #6 for this project. This is a dealbreaker for us.
* Testcases with errors show up expanded, detailing each failed test.
* Testcases without errors show up collapsed, but one can expand them on demand to inspect the details.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1128028/37510189-a38892ca-28fa-11e8-998d-837006053682.png)

Maybe this is out of the scope of `HtmlTestRunner`. I don't want to impose a personal preference, but rather share the features particular users have.

`ack` is just for the timing/debugging tools. Scoped commands are always safe to do after sending a `bind`, because they'll always arrive at the server after the `bind` (the websocket provides in-order reliable delivery of messages, and the connect-via-websocket state machines always sends a `bind` before announcing the connection to any other state machines, so there's no opportunity to send anything *before* the `bind`).

Sorry, I was not very clear when I created this issue. But I think this is different from #76.
#76 was talking about easy connection to a known person. So the possible way to solve this is 'reusable code' or 'specify a code'. And you pointed out keep connection may be a better way than using the same code.
And **this issue** is created when I try to figure out how to send from one to many. Actually, my suggestion does not mean using the same code from day to day. Besides, I believe a randomly generated code should only be valid for one 1-to-N transaction. The code is still short lived.

So I can think of two possible ways to improve this:
1. implemented an option like `--reuse-times times`. `times` should be equals to N. You can type the same code on N computers to receive this file. After finishing N transactions the sender should automatically close. Mostly, I believe this workflow can be done within half an hour, supposing the file size and N are relatively small.
2.  implemented an option like `--code-timeout mins`. And sender will allow all connections (restart waiting for connection using the same code, when a transaction is done) within the timeout. And automatically close when timeout.

Both ways above can make sure the code is short-lived when used properly. And you can improve the security by using both limits. For example, set default timeout to 30 mins when using `--reuse-times`. 
The only dangerous scene should happen when a stupid ass deliberately set both `--reuse-times infinite` and `--code-timeout infinite`. On that circumstance, he may want to use the wormhole as a Content Deliver Sevice. I think it's also OK to let him do that.

Hey @kamyarg this issue should be fixed. It was a vague, but represented the fact that Flask-Cors currently uses a heuristic to determine if the string should be treated as a regex or as a string literal for matching. Frankly that was probably a bad design from the get-go, but cannot easily be changed without breaking API compatibility.

For your issue, what is your goal? Sending an '*' as the origin is not allowed in combination with credwentials, as mentioned (See https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS "Credentialed requests and wildcards").

Instead if you use specify `supports_credentials=true`, with the defaults `CORS(origins='*', supports_credentials=true, send_wildcard=false)` it should work. Let me know if you have any issues.

Cheers,
Cory

Hello. Frankly both aren't good. Few points about both projects:
* `aioamqp` has a bad interface. It resolves purely around callbacks, and it's in the world of asyncio where we have `async for` and `async with` constructs...

* `asynqp` has potential (at least for me) in the API. It clearly states what's public and how to use things.

@jvmancuso  If you need code for doing constrained random sampling, I've written something that should work: 

```python
def constrainted_uniform(lower, upper, size, constraints,
                         random_state=np.random):
    """
    Example:
        >>> bounds = np.array([[0, 10], [0, 10]])
        >>> def cons1(data):
        >>>     # model constraint x <= y (true is any non-negative value)
        >>>     x, y = data
        >>>     return y - x
        >>> constraints = [
        >>>     {'type': 'leq', 'fun': cons1}
        >>> ]
        >>> lower = bounds[:, 0]
        >>> upper = bounds[:, 1]
        >>> size = (3, bounds.shape[0])
        >>> random_state = np.random.RandomState(0)
        >>> constrainted_uniform(lower, upper, size, constraints, random_state)
        array([[ 4.37587211,  8.91773001],
               [ 6.02763376,  5.44883183],
               [ 5.68044561,  9.25596638]])
    """
    def satisfied(cons, x_try):
        if cons['type'] == 'eq':
            return cons['fun'](x_try) == 0
        elif cons['type'] == 'leq':
            return cons['fun'](x_try) >= 0
        else:
            raise ValueError(cons['type'])

    # Initial random sample
    x_seeds = random_state.uniform(lower, upper, size=size)
    unsatisfied_idx, = np.where(
        [all(satisfied(cons, x_try) for cons in constraints)
                     for x_try in x_seeds])

    # Reject and resample until everything is satisfied
    max_iters = min(100, (2 * size[0]) ** 2)
    n_iters = 0
    while len(unsatisfied_idx) > 0:
        if n_iters > max_iters:
            raise RuntimeError('took too long to find good points')
        n_invalid = len(unsatisfied_idx)
        new_size = (n_invalid,) + tuple(size[1:])
        new_seeds = random_state.uniform(lower, upper, size=new_size)

        needs_resample = [
            not all(satisfied(cons, x_try) for cons in constraints)
            for x_try in new_seeds
        ]

        x_seeds[unsatisfied_idx] = new_seeds
        unsatisfied_idx = unsatisfied_idx[needs_resample]
        n_iters += 1
    return x_seeds
```

> You should instead call openByVendorIDAndProductID to get an opened

> Please see the documentation (pydoc usb1 or equivalent) for more details
> (and more variants).

> Python cannot find libusb. It must be installed where the dynamic library
> loader can find it. Please check libusb 1.0.x documentation (the underlying
> C library) on how to install it. Note that windows requires extra steps to
> be able to use devices, installing libusb 1.0.x itself is not enough.

To clarify, you want to avoid duplicating the error code(s) for files where the same errors are to be ignored? So that instead of ...

    foo.py: E100
    bar.py: E100

... you could write ...

    foo.py,bar.py: E100

Note that glob patterns are already supported. So for examples if all files with the same error to be ignored are located in the same directory (or have a common prefix) you could just do something like that:

    foo/*: E100
    test_*.py: E101

There might be scenarios though, in which glob patterns aren't powerful enough, but if you list multiple files in the same line, this line will likely become very long and hard to read.

I think the problem here is really in josepy....

https://github.com/certbot/josepy/blob/master/src/josepy/jwk.py#L107

```python
        try:
            key = cls._load_cryptography_key(data, password, backend)
        except errors.Error as error:
            logger.debug('Loading symmetric key, asymmetric failed: %s', error)
            return JWKOct(key=data)
```

where, despite the function description above it to the contrary, if it can't load the data as a public key it dubiously falls back to loading it as a symmetric key, which should always "work" but often not meaningfully.

Subsequent mozilla-django-oidc code then treats the result as a public key, not knowing any of the wiser...

The upshot is that for any incorrectly formatted public key, the failure doesn't occur until much later and is hard to diagnose.

> OK, so (still would like the full hash, as I can't repro your seg fault

>
> This .obj does indeed take forever. It is running, and so will eventually
> finish, but not for a while. The problem (or at least the first) is in
> objFileImporter.cpp:InternReadFile() It iterates through the buffer and
> tries to remove backslashes followed by newlines. If you open you .obj in a
> text editor and search for \, you'll find a bunch of them. They seem to
> occur when you have exported a quad intstead of triangle.
>
> So the code goes through the entire buffer checking for '\', removing it,
> then removing all immediate consecutive '\r' and '\n' chars. Your .obj uses
> \r\n for new lines. So we are doing 3 erases every time we find a '\'.
>
> The std::vector::erase function has a complexity of O(n), ie. for a 102MB
> file, very slow.
>
> This hunt for backslashes takes approx 1/6th of the total import + post
> process time (at least for my file), and I have no backslashes. So I've
> been wandering about this. However never had much of a reason to bother
> changing it.
>
> The ideal solution would be to parse and skip these chars in the actually
> parsing stage, therefore not having to do any erases.
>
> Another option would be to use a STL container that had O(1) complexity
> for removing items in the middle (ie. a list).
>
> As a quick test I removed the \\\r\n in notepad++ and then the file import
> took 4 minutes, and the entire thing about 5.5 minutes (my initial
> estimate). Unfortunately it then failed with "bad allocation". So I'll look

@crowdwave Assuming you were using `list_objects` I wasn't able to reproduce this. The API potentially returns more than `MaxItems` in a single API call but the paginator iterator should only yield up to `MaxItems` across all pages.

In general, you probably want to just use `PageSize` and not `MaxItems`. If you are absolutely positive that you want to set a `MaxItems` you are almost always better off using [build_full_result](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/paginate.py#L288) which understands truncating results outside of a page boundary.

Upgrading requests is generally a high risk, low reward task. So if we aren't directly benefiting from a new feature or bug fix, it generally isn't worth the risk. However, `2.10` did add the ability to use SOCKS proxies, which is an [open feature request](https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/880).

Even then, we likely won't be able to update past `2.11.1` because they added a feature to attempt to rewind file-like objects on certain kinds of errors, which is something we implemented ourselves some time ago. Balancing those conflicting implementations will be very risky.
Closing, PR #827 was merged which fixes this issue.

Module  | Inputs | Outputs
----------  | --------- | ----------
`auth.py` |  `str` **only** | `str`
`endpoint.py` | `str` or `bytes` | `str` or `bytes`
`serialize.py` | mostly `str`, but `bytes` work for blob types | `str`

The `auth.py` module simply doesn't support bytes as input. It makes too many assumptions about the input being a string. This PR changes that to allow support for byte bodies. The `endpoint.py` module passes things through as-is, but this PR is changing that to always send `bytes` to the HTTP library.

The `serialize.py` module is interesting. It only supports `bytes` in very specific cases and fails when they are passed elsewhere (e.g. a parameter of type `string` will fail to serialize when passed `b'hello'`). I'm not sure if we want to support this use-case.

Given all that, I still think this is a good change to make to support people with PyOpenSSL installed.

The error was changed, since the text we had up to 0.21 was the first error message, which was expected to trigger when we had in fact not implemented support for https or ssh. It's been changed to say the url is unuspported.

The message however doesn't mention a particular dependency because that part of libgit2 does not know about the particular dependency. All it knows is whether there is a supported url schema or not, and libssh2 is just the default way to get ssh.

The problem with the mention of libssh2 is that doing the obvious thing (installing libssh2) won't change anything, and it's just one possible dependency. You also need OpenSSL to use https but that's not mentioned. Soon (hopefully) you might also want GnuTLS or SecureTransport to use https. Are we going to mention everything in the hopes the user guesses they need to install the dev package and rebuild libgit2 from that?

Getting the dependencies in the right place is something that needs to happen during the installation of libgit2, which the package manager might handle but if not, these dependencies are something you would want

https://github.com/pinterest/pymemcache/blob/master/pymemcache/client/base.py#L887

So this line will never be executed by default:
https://github.com/pinterest/pymemcache/blob/master/pymemcache/client/base.py#L270
Thus nullify the meaning of setting the  `default_noreply` arg.

I think the default value of `noreply` of the `PooledClient` APIs should be `None` just as the `Client` ones do.

@harshavardhana 

> In this can we make release_conn() to be close() api basically treating get_object returned as an FD.
> and then a user closes either with explicit close() . Extra polating from this someone can do
> 
> with client.get_object('my-bucketname', 'my-objectname') as obj_reader:
> 
> and close be handled automatically? - release_conn() is kind of non-standard and hard to follow. I wonder why urllib3 cannot do this ideally.
> 
> Thoughts?

Turns out this is not worth the trouble - the [response module](https://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/index.html#module-urllib3.response) is already an instance io.IOBase, and supports with `with` statement with the meaning that it will call `close()` on the response object - the [close()](https://github.com/shazow/urllib3/blob/master/urllib3/response.py#L474) method closes the connection without returning it to the pool. 

Calling `release_conn()` is not strictly necessary unless the user is particular about re-using the connection - otherwise there is no leakage of resources - when the object is garbage collected, the connection is simply closed.

just catching up on the compression protocol: 

From the docs:

Kafka supports compressing messages for additional efficiency, however this is more complex than just compressing a raw message. Because individual messages may not have sufficient redundancy to enable good compression ratios, compressed messages must be sent in special batches (although you may use a batch of one if you truly wish to compress a message on its own). The messages to be sent are wrapped (uncompressed) in a MessageSet structure, which is then compressed and stored in the Value field of a single "Message" with the appropriate compression codec set. The receiving system parses the actual MessageSet from the decompressed value. The outer MessageSet should contain only one compressed "Message" (see KAFKA-1718 for details).
Kafka currently supports two compression codecs with the following codec numbers:


makes sense you would want to batch messages to give the compression algo a chance to actually do something. But it feels like an overloaded protocol, one `Message` made up of many `messages`, meh.

My first reaction is to limit the size of messagesets sent to the a compressed message set to 1 megabyte(kafka's default). Allow users to override. We batch the messages by the sum of the uncompressed size, this wouldn't maximize the compression and bandwidth but would keep the producer code simpler. Trying to guess the size of the compressed message a priori seems nasty. 

The other approach is we could be optimistic and keep the current approach, pass in as many messages all possible and then check the compressed size, throw and error if it's too big and then pass in a smaller set. This would be better for smaller messages as long as the total compressed Message is small enough, but would create an overhead for larger messages.

A mix of these two approaches i think would be the fastest but the most complex. Basically the producer would check the size of some enqueued messages and switch between the defensive and optimistic approach based on size.

I have fun working on kafka-python. I hope others enjoy it. If there's a better driver out there, huzzah!

My one piece of advice is that all of you should get more involved on the kafka-dev mailing list, particularly with respect to API KIPs and wire protocol issues. The core team is very focused on the java ecosystem, and that can lead to api designs that force client drivers into less than ideal positions. If nothing else, getting more non-java perspectives into the mix would be a great improvement.

I know Magnus is working very hard on the librdkafka side and it does appear that Confluent is paying a few more people to work on wrapper libraries. That will be a great benefit for users that want higher performance than they can get from a single python process w/ GIL restrictions.

But I do think one of the great benefits of a community is a diversity of approaches and view points. And python is in a very unique position because we can generally develop faster than other languages and I believe we should continue to leverage that to the benefit of the entire community.

So with that said, yay for options! Have fun writing software. We only live once.

Hi @GeekMe93,

We use row/col coordinates throughout scikit-image, with a few exceptions. The order of input coordinates is correct currently. If you work with x, y coordinates, you need to convert manually before passing them to scikit-image.

See the documentation for more info:

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/numpy_images.html#coordinate-conventions

@jmetz i believe the "correct" answer which @stonebig is seeking could be the the Feret diameter or something like it (e.g. PR #1780) which does a point-by-point evaluation of all the diameters of an object as-is. for rectangles & squares, this would return a corner-to-corner distance. modifying the function in that PR to return the _minimum_ distance would give a cross-section pretty close to the short distance of the rectangle edge:

```
def feret_diameter_minimum(self):
    # self: a RegionProp object
    from ..morphology.convex_hull import convex_hull_image
    label_image = self._label_image
    label = self.label
    identity_convex_hull = convex_hull_image(label_image == label)
    coordinates = np.vstack(find_contours(identity_convex_hull, 0.5, 
                                              fully_connected = 'high'))
    distances = pdist(coordinates, 'sqeuclidean')
    return sqrt(np.min(distances))
```

alternatively, given the RegionProp.major_axis_length and RegionProp.orientation, one can compute the *actual* length of these cross-sections with something like this (some values like`1linewidth=2` were specific to my application), which in the rectangle case is parallel to the sides of the object:

```
def get_lw(rps, b_image):
    # rps: the RegionProp object for an object
    # b_images: binary image of the object in question
    # i think b_image could be replaced by rps._label_image
    # but it has been a while since i worked i this code
    y0, x0 = rps.centroid
    cosorient = math.cos(rps.orientation)
    sinorient = math.sin(rps.orientation)

    x1 = x0 + cosorient * 0.5 * rps.major_axis_length
    y1 = y0 - sinorient * 0.5 * rps.major_axis_length
    x2 = x0 - cosorient * 0.5 * rps.major_axis_length
    y2 = y0 + sinorient * 0.5 * rps.major_axis_length

    l_profile = np.array(profile_line(b_image, (y1, x1), (y2, x2), 
                                      linewidth = 2, 
                                      order = 0)).astype('float64')
    l_imp = (l_profile.sum() * rps.major_axis_length)/float(len(l_profile))

    x1 = x0 - sinorient * 0.5 * rps.minor_axis_length
    y1 = y0 - cosorient * 0.5 * rps.minor_axis_length
    x2 = x0 + sinorient * 0.5 * rps.minor_axis_length
    y2 = y0 + cosorient * 0.5 * rps.minor_axis_length

    w_profile = np.array(profile_line(b_image, (y1, x1), (y2, x2), 
                                      linewidth = 2, 
                                      order = 0)).astype('float64')
    w_imp = (w_profile.sum() * rps.minor_axis_length)/float(len(w_profile))
    
    return (l_imp, w_imp)
```

I have worked a lot with video in the last months and I would have some suggestions. 

- working with video is tricky (codecs are a real mess) and *nothing* should be assumed to be working correctly. Everything needs to be tested, if possible with a video in which each frame is identifiable. A way to get such a video is with the following bash script:

::

    #!/bin/bash
    
    # Use imagemagick to generate an image sequence containing numbers 0..999
    for i in $(seq -f "%05g" 0 999)
    do
            convert -size 51x18 -pointsize 16 -font /usr/share/fonts/truetype/freefont/FreeMono.ttf label:$i $i.png
    done

    # Use ffmpeg to create a video from the image sequence:
    ffmpeg -y -f image2 -r 30 -i "%05d.png" -r 30 test.avi 

- Problems I noticed in particular are:
    - frame accurate seek
    - missing frames at the beginning or end of the video stream

I dont know how openCV deals with this issues now, but some time ago the seek in particular was unreliable. Test it before using it!

A good ffmpeg wrapper I found and used for some time is `ffvideo` (https://bitbucket.org/zakhar/ffvideo/). Even this is a good wrapper it had some issues with accurate seek, reading the first or last frames (depending on the codec), and performance within threads. I forked it and reimplemented the decoding part following the ffmpeg tutorial. I would highly appreciate if people would test my version and improve/use it. The author of the original version seems to have abandoned his project. I am happy to manage further bugfixing if needed and try again to send him a pull request once I am  sure my version does not break something I have overlooked.

You can find my fork on https://bitbucket.org/groakat/ffvideo . I will also put it up on `conda` together with the `ffmpeg` dependencies win64, osx and linux in the next couple of days. Especially for unexperienced users, installing ffmpeg and sorts can be quite painful. With `conda` the installation becomes really easy.

For anyone who stumbles across this, you can get swarming to work by setting the `NUPIC` env variable manually to the package location. If you installed nupic via pip, you can simply use: `export NUPIC="$(pip show nupic | grep "Location:" | sed 's/Location: //')/nupic"`

If you try to run an example (like me) under 0.2.1, please also note that support for relative file paths is in the swarming spec is broken in this version. Use absolute paths instead and you should be fine.

In retrospect, `pip install --upgrade --ignore-installed setuptools wheel` should never have made it into `ci/bamboo/build-linux.sh` (I put it there.  I'm sorry).  In the bamboo builds, `get-pip.py` is used to install `pip`, `setuptools`, and `wheel` -- see https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing/#installing-with-get-pip-py for official recommended installation technique.  We're vendoring in `get-pip.py` (see https://github.com/numenta/nupic/tree/master/ci/bamboo) for a number of reasons, one of which is to version lock a known, good version of `pip`, `setuptools`, and `wheel`.  Calling `pip install --upgrade --ignore-installed setuptools wheel` defeats the purpose of bundling `get-pip.py`.

Instead, if we need to update `pip`, `setuptools`, or `wheel`, we should update `get-pip.py` (and document the versions for anyone not using the correct installation method).

Let me say upfront that I have no problems merging the projects, however, we have at least two issues:

* `pytest-catchlog` is meant to capture logging while `pytest-logging` is meant to display logging; Considering only the names of the plugins, `pytest-logging` is broad enough to include capturing logs, while `catchlog` is not broad enough to make users think that is also takes care of displaying the log messages(not really a big problem and I'm probably biased towards my plugin name :smile:)
* Licensing.`pytest-logging` was created using the human resources of [SaltStack](saltstack.com)(me and my time), and while SaltStack has no issues with open source projects(it actually contributes back to a lot of them), it releases them using the Apache 2.0 license.

Would you be interested in merging both plugins but under `pytest-logging` instead(since it's a broader name)? We would actually merge repositories so that no git history is lost and all of the catchlog contributors hard work is given due credit; we could even move the repository to [`pytest-dev`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest)
Would you mind dual licensing the plugin in order to keep my contribution to the plugin under Apache 2.0?

If the answer to both the above questions is no, I'm sure we can still work something out but I'll need to bring this matter to [SaltStack](saltstack.com) to get permission to release my work under the MIT license.

It's true that rdflib's stores (and probably some parsers and serializers) are in general not preserving the insert order of triples.

The reason for this is that we're talking about graphs composed of `(s, p, o)` triples. As the data-model doesn't have a field for `n-th triple`, it would be weird to require the underlying stores to invent one. Also relying on that order would be highly confusing (if not wrong) and inconsistent for example with SPARQL where there are no guarantees about the order either.

So unless you really have a convincing use-case i guess the priority of this is quite low.

yupp, i see the same issues... #609 was about the constructor, while this PR in general tries to improve subsequent `append` calls by giving `Collection` a state, which might cause problems later on.

What about giving `append` an optional arg `_end`? It would be a private arg to indicate that its use is potentially dangerous if you don't know what you're doing. If set it, `append` would trust it to be the end-pointer and we could use it to provide an O(n) `extend` method, which we could also use in the ctor?

however, we could already start to check on 'modern' python2 code (whithout being fully python3 compatible, but making it so that less changes will be needed in the future, and without extra dependencies on `future` or `six`
 futurize's stage1 looks promissing , we could have vsc-install run this as part of the tests, and require 
that all our code is modern python.

We are already enabling more and more pylint checks every month or so, so we could later on add the futurize stage1 as another check.
http://python-future.org/futurize.html#forwards-conversion-stage1

what futurize stage1 does:
```
Modernize Python 2 code only; no compatibility with

Detecting this reliably is the hard part. I don't think there is any other way to detect this externally besides parsing PostgreSQL logs. Parsing logs is a huge can of worms because the logs might go into files in arbitrary location, stderr or syslog. I think it's also possible to get these messages when a streaming host falls too far behind, then switches over to pulling from the archive, switching back to streaming once it has caught up.

A thought I had was to have a small proxy daemon that speaks PostgreSQL replication protocol, the database would then connect locally to this daemon and would get proxied to the correct master. The proxy can then forward relevant error messages to Patroni and can also be used to change primary_conninfo without restarting the standby. I have actually written one that does the proxying part and could be extended to publish the error information.

Another idea is to make Patroni expose xlog dir contents over the API. If a standby is not able to connect to master Patroni could then compare recovery point on standby to xlog available on the master to determine if it has fallen hopelessly behind and should reinit. There are a bunch of nuances about how to figure the recovery point out when you can't connect to the standby because it hasn't reached consistent recovery point yet.

And to top this all off, if archiving is enabled you would need a way to poll the archive to see if recovery is still feasible.

Seems like a whole bunch of work complicated work for something that is reasonably well solved by monitoring for replication lag and having an admin manually poke Patroni to reinit if it seems the right way to solve the issue. Reinit is not always the correct option anyway, you want to avoid reiniting a terabyte database if you can avoid it by using rsync. And having the standby give up buys little else besides a reduction in log spam.

    import dynet as dy

The convention is Python is that private or internal portions of the code are indicated by prefixing the class, function, method or module with an [underscore](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#public-and-internal-interfaces).  Since the **MdvFile** class does not have this prefix it should be considered in the public API which users have access to.  That said, the class is not imported into the pyart.io namespace nor included in the [User Reference Manual](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/io.html) and so it not as public as say the `read_mdv` function.  

I've always treated the file specific classes, **MdvFile**, **SigmetFile**, **NEXRADLevel2File**, as accessible to "power users" who want to more complete control over the specific file and do not need the other functionality and niceties that Py-ART provides with the **Radar**class.

All that is a long way of saying; I think it is fine to change the implementation details of the **MdvFile** class as is done here as long as `read_mdv` is update to use the new implementation.  Power users can still use the class for their own use, such as plotting MDV files without creating a **Radar** object, but should not be surprised or upset at this or future changes as the class as it is not documented in the User Manual.  

As for depreciating **MdvDisplay** you are correct, the proper method is to add a depreciation warning, wait a release and then remove the class.  Although I doubt the class is widely used, since it is documented in the User Manual and used in the Example this is the path we will follow.  Once you have finished up the modification to the **MdvFile** I can make the necessary changes to **MdvDisplay** and add the depreciation bits.

@dreyco676 , note that there's some ambiguity with the name PyKerberos. The original library produced by Apple is:

- called PyKerberos upstream (Issue Tracker, etc.), 
- called `kerberos` on PyPI,
- and often packaged as `python-kerberos` by Linux distros.

During the four years that PyKerberos went without an update, some unknown person uploaded a package called `pykerberos` to PyPI (with some improvements), leading a lot of people to mistake this for the original package.

Since then, the Apple version has released an update (version 1.2.2) that includes fixes, like my Python 3 patch. Since the PyPI `pykerberos` so far doesn't have a source repo or issue tracker, I would suggest staying with `kerberos`. They're only interchangeable because they're APIs happen to be the same, no other Kerberos libraries can function for requests-kerberos at the moment.

I ran into this situation. The refresh is attempted, and the provider replied with an error stating that the refresh token had expired. oauthlib pulled the error description out of the reply from the provider and raised an exception. This exception was raised here:

https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py#L383

To automate this, you would need something like what is needed here:

https://github.com/requests/requests-oauthlib/issues/246

In other words, if both the access token and refresh token are expired, you will need to go back through the process of obtaining a new set. If using a grant such as client credentials, this can be done automatically.

@tswast Thanks for the report!  As it sits today, `QueryResults` is intended to model a synchronous query:  it surfaces the `job` property only once that has been populated from the server during its `run()` or `fetch_data()` methods.

It looks as thought we need to split out the part which initiates the synchronous query and the bits which allow fetching the results, to make it clearer how the two are supposed to interact, and how to reuse the second bit with an async query job.

The synchronous case would look something like:

```python
query = client.sync_query(QUERY_STRING)
query.timeout_ms = 30000
query.run()

if not query.complete:
    wait_for_job(query.job)

# if we know the result set is small
for row in query.rows:
    do_something_with(row)

#otherwise, page through them
results = query.results()

rows, total_count, token = results.fetch_data()

while True:
    for row in rows:
        do_something_with(row)
    if token is None:
        break
    rows, total_count, token = results.fetch_data(
        page_token=token)
```

The asyncronous case would look like:

```python
job = client.async_query(QUERY_STRING)
job.begin()

wait_for_job(job)

results = job.results()

rows, total_count, token = results.fetch_data()

while True:
    for row in rows:
        do_something_with(row)
    if token is None:
        break
    rows, total_count, token = results.fetch_data(
        page_token=token)
```

What should the goals be for this?
I would love make a shortcut to access the transcript data more easily but I think that some the streaming interim results make that difficult.  The other data that's contextual to the responses should be easy to access as well.

Here's my proposal for a strategy.

1. Make sync and async recognize return results with the transcripts available directly from the result, since there should be only one result (`result.transcript`, `result.confidence`).  Then alternatives can be on `result.alternatives[0].transcript` etc.

2. Streaming recognize returns many different kinds of responses (see example below).  I think we could wrap these responses and just `yield` them to the consumer. Then you could have things like `result.audio_started`, `result.speech_started`(this implies tracking some state though). Then you could have something similar to sync and async where you have the top transcript available directly on the `result` but other data could be there as well but it could be deeper.

3. If we added back that result container object that I setup, then we could offer the consumer a method they could poll to get the best over all guess for the response so far. Which in the example below, would be appending the top result for each `result_index`.

Example at this point 
```
results { alternatives { transcript: " that is" } stability: 0.9 } results { alternatives { transcript: " the question" } stability: 0.01 } result_index: 1
```
you could call `results.get_best_text() = 'to be or not to be that is`.

Example of accessing data for 
```
results { alternatives { transcript: " that is" } stability: 0.9 } results { alternatives { transcript: " the question" } stability: 0.01 } result_index: 1
```

```python
>>> print(result.transcript)
 that is
>>> print(result.alternatives[0].transcript)
 the question
```

Unfortunately the docs are structured such that each file format needs 3 or 4 samples specific to that file type.

- [Avro](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-cloud-storage-avro) 3 samples: load (schema defined by filetype), load append, load truncate
- [Parquet](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-cloud-storage-parquet) 3 samples: load (schema defined by filetype), load append, load truncate
- [CSV](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-cloud-storage-csv) 4 samples: load with explicit schema, load with autodetect, load append, load truncate
- [JSON](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-cloud-storage-json) 4 samples: load with explicit schema, load with autodetect, load append, load truncate
- [Datastore Backup](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-cloud-datastore) 3 samples: load (schema defined by filetype), load append, load truncate

That said, I bet we could get away with 1 or 2 samples for each if we reduce the append/truncate samples to their bare minimum and assume they can follow the other example to figure out where the job config should go.

```py
job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig()
job_config.write_disposition = bigquery.WriteDisposition.WRITE_APPEND
```

```py
job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig()
job_config.write_disposition = bigquery.WriteDisposition.WRITE_TRUNCATE
```

I can meet you 1/2 way and describe exactly what needs to be done.  That should make it easier for you or someone else who hacks on pants regularly and is annoyed to put their money where their annoyance is:

The fundamental issue today is that the `pantsbuild.pants` artifact includes jvm and python backends and installs everything from each.  Combined with the current RoundEngine this leads to extra Tasks being run when they are not needed.

To free python from the jvm the easiest short-term fix that does not incur debt is to isolate 3 packages:

1. pants core (enough to load plugins)
2. [pants.backend.python](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/tree/master/src/python/pants/backend/python)
3. all other backends

Now the targets are all currently hyper-fine grained - so no issue there - its just a few `provides=setup_py(...)` clauses that need to be added in the right spot to break [this](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/BUILD#L14) into 1-3 above.

With these 2 BUILD file edits complete and an update to the release script [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/build-support/bin/release.sh#L9) we can publish `pantsbuild.pants.python` and you could consume that sdist instead of the pantsbuild.pants fat sdist with jvm stuff in it.

The behavior of the CLI parameter did not change. Namely, when you specify the parameter sockeye will decode the given number of sentences in a separate decoder process. If `--optimized-metric` is set to bleu as well that bleu will be used for early stopping. Otherwise the BLEU scores are just reported but early stopping is done based on the perplexity values.

I happened to run into the same nested SAM template issue a couple of days ago. Although it would be great if SAM templates would be expanded automatically by CloudFormation, for now I use [sam-translate.py](/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/bin/sam-translate.py) as workaround locally and for deployment.

1. Package SAM template to make sure all `CodeUri` point to S3 urls since `aws-sam-translator` doesn't support local uris.
2. Expand the packaged template using [`sam-translate.py`](/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/bin/sam-translate.py)
3. Upload expanded template to S3 bucket.
4. Deploy the root stack, providing the bucket the templates have been uploaded to.


## Steps

I didn't have `aws-sam-translator` installed so the following worked for me.

    pip install docopt PyYAML aws-sam-translator

As far as I can tell [`sam-translate.py`](/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/bin/sam-translate.py) itself doesn't get installed so create a copy of it on my machine.

    sam package
      --s3-bucket "$BUCKET" \
      --template-file "$TEMPLATE_DIR/my-app.sam.yaml" \
      --output-template-file "$TEMPLATE_DIR/my-app.sam.pkg.yaml"

    python sam-translate.py \
      --input-file="$TEMPLATE_DIR/my-app.sam.pkg.yaml" \
      --output-file="$TEMPLATE_DIR/my-app.cfn.json"

    aws s3 cp --recursive "$TEMPLATE_DIR" "s3://$BUCKET/templates"

    aws cloudformation deploy --template-file ./my-root-stack.yaml

### Example of the root stack

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: 2010-09-09

Description: Nested stack featuring SAM

Parameters:
  TemplateBucket:
    Type: String
    Default: deployment-artifacts
    Description: |
      The bucket for the CloudFormation templates and packaged SAM application.

Resources:
  MyAppStack:
    Type: AWS::CloudFormation::Stack
    Properties:
      TemplateURL: !Sub https://s3.amazonaws.com/${TemplateBucket}/templates/my-app.cfn.json
```

Following a quick search through the Google Drive API, this doesn't seem to be (easily) possible. The API does not seem to expose this information explicitly. 

According to the docs, there is a `labels` array which may contain this information. You can access the labels with `FetchMetadata(all_fields=True)` like so:

```python
file1 = drive.CreateFile({'id': '<some file ID here>'})

file1.FetchMetadata(fetch_all=True)  # fetch_all=True downloads all metadata fields.
# Alternatively, you download the 'labels' field only.
file1.FetchMetadata(fields='labels')
print(file1['labels'])
```

All the field names are documented in the official Google Drive API documentation:
https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/reference/files

PyDrive metadata functions are documented here:
https://pythonhosted.org/PyDrive/filemanagement.html#download-file-metadata-from-file-id

> Also, sorry about all the deleted migrations. What's the correct procedure for those? I made new ones at one point because of some auth errors I had.

Correct procedure is not deleting them. :)

Generating new ones is problematic, because others who have already run the old ones will have DBs that think it's already run the ones with that name, and hence won't run the new ones you generate, which may now be different.

If you could "undelete" the old ones (either manually recreating the files verbatim, or I think there's a git command to reverse the effects of a commit).

Yes, because the container may have written to the mounted volume that still exists as a temp dir on the host that we still want to clean up. Not only that, we typically want to upload those files back to girder after the container exits, so we *need* them to outlive the container process.

The reason we have to clean them up using another docker image is because the docker engine writes those files with owner=root, and we prescribe not running the docker client as root.

```python
    def tail(self, obs=5, maxintwidth=14):
        """
        display the last n rows of a table

        :param obs: the number of rows of the table that you want to display. The default is 5
	:param maxintsize: the maximum width of the integer format for total observations. The default is 14 (format=bestd14.) 
        :return:
        """
        if maxintwidth > 32:
            maxintwidth = 32
        code = ("proc sql;select count(*) format=best" + str(maxintwidth)+ '.' + " into :lastobs from " + \
                self.libref + '.' + self.table + self._dsopts() + ";%put lastobs=&lastobs tom;quit;")

        nosub = self.sas.nosub
        self.sas.nosub = False
