Starting several instances of the daemon is not really feasible in my current configuration, but I may find a way to filter messages out, based on the group each neighbor is in (another simple way would be to augment the messages syntax with a "group": "name" field).

asyncio is a completely new codebase, and has less footprint than Twisted (but also less features, knobs, etc - but those are usually not needed nowerdays with AB Py clients).

Yeah, you don't need separate embeddings for this.  In SQuAD, we have a paragraph and a question that receive different padding lengths, but share their embedding layers.  You just create two different `TextField` objects, with difference keys in the instance, and padding is done _per key_ in the `Instance` object.

We build the `Instance` objects in the dataset reader, with the relevant lines here:

https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/b12517c78db605eda815a0468fd92c014e37821b/allennlp/data/dataset_readers/squad.py#L173-L175

Then, in the model code, we just use the same `TextFieldEmbedder` to embed the two different fields:

https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/b12517c78db605eda815a0468fd92c014e37821b/allennlp/models/bidaf.py#L186-L187

I'm pretty sure the code already does what you want it to.  The only time you'd want two _different_ embedders is if you wanted to, e.g., use character-level information for the question, but not the passage.  We could in principle do this, but sharing the embedder is currently not possible when building things `from_params`.

I was able to create some files on a test S3 endpoint to prove out that the path munging is not safe, and, equally importantly, that what we have today works.

Create the following files on an S3 endpoint:
- `/~`
- `/~/\/~` (`\` is a dir name, since S3 is a funky place)
- `/~/~/~/~`

---

Old Version of `ls -r /` | New Version of `ls -r /`
---|---
`~/` | `~/`
`~` | `~`
`~/\/` | `\/`
`~/~/` | `~/`
`~/\/~` | `\/~`
`~/~/~/` | `~/`
`~/~/~/~` | `~`

Though the old `ls -r` output isn't pretty (how could it be in this case?) it's correct.

---

Closely related to this is a question of whether `globus ls` should behave more like `ls` or like `find`. The `find`-style output is easier on us, as maintainers, as we don't have to munge the output very much at all.

@jaswilli, @corpulentcoffee, do you have strong opinions about our `ls` command being `find`-like vs. `ls`-like? (As in, would producing output like `find` offend your sensibilities?)

As far as I know, there is no other way _currently_.
However, I think there probably should be one.

> Another way could be via doing a dummy transfer with same source and destination, because a transfer task creates parent directories.

This is an interesting point. I took a look through the way we make these directories, and if I'm reading it right we're actually doing the simple loop over parent directories.
However, since it's happening just between Globus and the endpoint -- leaving out the overhead of multiple requests -- it's much faster than trying to do this with `mkdir` calls to the API.

As far as I know, GridFTP doesn't support a `mkdir -p` equivalent, so there's no simple fix.
We could expose the (reasonably fast) recursive mkdir operation through the Transfer API, but it probably isn't trivial -- at the very least, it could be slow enough when given very long paths which blocks and eventually times out. I'll open this up for discussion with the Transfer API team.

> For example I was thinking to do a transfer from "/dev/null" to ".dummy". However access to /dev/null is prohibited at some endpoints (eg. NERSC-DTN).

You could move an empty file to `.../.dummy` without using `/dev/null`, since most endpoints will disallow access to `/dev/`.
i.e. Your approach works, so long as you use a normal empty file.

---

Given that there is no viable work item against the SDK to derive from this feature request, I'm going to close this issue. However, I'll comment on it further if I get interesting info from discussing this with the Transfer API team.

How about, with `4.3`, we communicate a new way to install conda.  We'd do this loudly and obnoxiously to make sure people got the message.  We can also make it more programatic and extensible so that it hopefully wouldn't have to change for years to come.

I've found two examples in my shell init, and I'm sure there are many others we could cheat off of.

```
# Load RVM into a shell session *as a function*
[[ -s "$HOME/.rvm/scripts/rvm" ]] && source "$HOME/.rvm/scripts/rvm"
```

and

```
export RBENV_ROOT=/usr/local/var/rbenv
if which rbenv > /dev/null; then eval "$(rbenv init -)"; fi
```

The version I believe was corrected to match what it actually should be, which is `3.0`. However, there are still some packages that require the old version so it is kind of a mess.

So, I think there are few more issues that can occur than just these.

Case 1 can fail due to a higher build number or version resulting in a feature not being respected ( https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1334 ).

Case 2 can fail in another way, as well. Suppose you have a package that is dependent on SciPy and a particular BLAS. If there is no way to install these without features, it fails entirely to resolve. ( https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1476 ) If it is possible to install both of these without features, they will be installed without features. It should now be safe to update conda. This is fixed in the following python packages for linux-64:

* python-3.6.2-h0b30769_14.tar.bz2
* python-2.7.14-h931c8b0_15.tar.bz2
* python-2.7.13-hac47a24_15.tar.bz2
*python-3.5.4-hc053d89_14.tar.bz2

The issue was that we (Anaconda, formerly Continuum) build all of our packages with a new GCC package that we created using crosstool-ng. This package does not have gcc, it has a prefixed gcc - the missing command you're seeing, x86_64-conda_cos6-linux-gnu-gcc. This gets baked into python, and any extension built with that python goes looking for that compiler. We have fixed the issue using the _PYTHON_SYSCONFIGDATA_NAME variable that was added to python 3.6. We have backported that to python 2.7 and 3.5. You'll now only ever see python using default compilers (gcc), and you must set the _PYTHON_SYSCONFIGDATA_NAME to the appropriate filename to have the new compilers used. Setting this variable is something that we'll put into the activate scripts for the compiler package, so you'll never need to worry about it. It may take us a day or two to get new compiler packages out, though, so post issues on the conda-build issue tracker if you'd like to use the new compilers and need help getting started.

Relevant code changes are at:

py27: https://github.com/anacondarecipes/python-feedstock/tree/master-2.7.14
py35: https://github.com/anacondarecipes/python-feedstock/tree/master-3.5
py36: https://github.com/anacondarecipes/python-feedstock

Regarding this one, I think I now understand what's going on.

When performing the command `conda create -n test-pkg test=1.0`, conda's _first_ priority is to obtain the latest version and build of the packages specified on the command line. In this case, that means it is going to favor `test-1.0-1`. Since the only version of the package with this version and build number is the debug version, it _must_ install that one. 

The bug here is that features are never supposed to be pulled in involuntarily. So conda shouldn't be allowed to select the feature in that case.

Now, if you give both packages the _same_ build number, `conda create -n test-pkg test=1.0` now has a choice. There are two packages with the same version and build number, one with the `debug` feature, and one without. In this case, conda _always_ picks the one with the fewest features. So the non-debug version wins. Again, that's correct behavior.

If you want to force the debug feature in that second case, you must do `conda create -n test-pkg test=1.0 debug`. That will guarantee that the `debug` version of the package is installed. 

So the lesson here is that if you want to avoid strange behavior, the build numbers need to line up.

However, I can understand why that would be confusing. Frankly, features _are_ confusing, and brittle, and I would prefer we not use them, and find other ways to implement our requirements.

Thanks Francesc. Yes zarr has support for synchronizing write operations to
an array. Multiple threads or processes can safely append and/or update the
same array, some more info here:

Zarr does not have any table abstraction, however, you would have to work
directly with arrays.

@slaweksiluk The issue with the simple probability is the unbound delay. What's the chance for 100 cycles delay? strobe_prob^100. What's the chance for infinite delay? strobe_prob^infinity - this is not zero.
Written that way it seems a ridiculous concern. However, if I want to verify that the delay is modelled correctly, there is always the chance for an outlier that doesn't match. A probability of 0.5 as in your example is interpreted as every second cycle in average. However, it also allows a case where you have no action for 1000 cycles. For a test case running 64 cycles, this will break the check. If you give a min and max for the latency, there is no such extreme outlier.
Certainly, a check based on the average can still break. However, it is now possible to do hard checks with the min and the max. You cannot do this with a simple probability.

This obviously also applies for the AXI slave probabilities.

This is a duplicate of #831 

You need the `ignore_empty_list` option .  There are two kinds of exceptions:

1. **Something failed with either Curator or Elasticsearch.** This is covered by the `continue_if_exception` option.
2. **The list to act on is empty.** This is covered by the `ignore_empty_list` option.

The reason these are separate is that there are cases where an empty list will occur, but it shouldn't be treated as a regular exception.  Because Curator can catch an empty list as a separate exception, it won't accidentally continue if a type 1 exception was the cause (because in such a case, you wouldn't normally want it to continue).  

Empty list conditions can occur when you first start using Curator.  You might want to purge indices older than 30 days, but you only have 3 or 4 right now.  This would trigger the exception above, but it's not really an error.  This is why the separate `ignore_empty_list` option exists.

While a bit of a strange command, I believe it does work as intended. 

You cannot create the URL path map (the containing type) without having at least one rule. If you could, this command would simply create an empty map and you would then fill it with calls to `application-gateway url-path-map rule create`. 

Thus this command has to create the "first rule". According to the URL path map rule object structure, a single rule can have multiple paths. That is why paths accepts multiple strings. You cannot create multiple rules with this command, only the first rule. Subsequent rules must be added with calls to `application-gateway url-path-map rule create`.

One thing I've thought about is extending the caching implementations to optionally support a "fetch since" system with arbitrary "since-ness" stored by `machine`.

For example, the HTTP cacher could store the `Date` response header of the HTTP download request and send the `If-Modified-Since` on subsequent requests. That way, the server could tell the cacher that nothing has changed and the cacher could tell the rest of the system that nothing has changed.

Another example would be the Esri cacher. Some layers support a [`returnUpdates`](http://resources.arcgis.com/en/help/rest/apiref/layer.html) feature that we might be able to use to only return rows that have changed since the last time we processed the source.

And of course the 'fallback' case would be that we always cache and process everything like we do now.

A few issues:

- This is listed as a `language: python` hook, which means it gets its own virtual environment but I don't see `mypy` being added to `setup.py` to install it
- I believe `mypy` needs access to all of the installed dependencies in order to do proper type inference, as such it's better suited for either:
    - A test (line in `tox.ini`) where all dependencies are available
    - A `local` hook which assumes the virtualenv is active (technically, this is what is happening with the definition in this PR by luck), similar to the example given for `pylint` [here](http://pre-commit.com/#repository-local-hooks)
- If it is *intentionally* depending on an activated virtualenv, the `language: system` type is more appropriate (and doesn't require a noop virtualenv)
- imo duplicating docs about `files` and `args` (and `exclude`) is unnecessary in the README, this is covered in the [pre-commit framework documentation itself](http://pre-commit.com)
- It may be better to contribute to / use [existing mypy support for pre-commit](https://github.com/jerome-baek/pre-commit-mypy)

Hi @pfreixes, sorry for confusion. I was not aware this issue is about the server-side tracing. 
Indeed my comment was regarding the outgoing traffic, so the requests done with `ClientSession`. 

Kindly point me what is the right way to make suggestions about extending that feature. Should I open another issue ? 

I'm aware of tracing mechanism and I that I can pass the `trace_request_ctx` to `get`/`post`/`put`/`delete` methods. However I don't think it's very practical. Please consider a situation when a third-party library is used, which wraps the individual http calls into an application-logic methods. Take `boto` as an example. It doesn't provide access to individual http requests, it exposes services specific to AWS resources. To be able to use `TraceConfig` with variables passed from the context, `boto` would have to update it's interfaces to optionally take in `trace_request_ctx` on all the calls and pass it to underlying methods of `ClientSession`. It clearly will not happen.

On the other hand I can see a way to have context variables in the TraceConfig, but it's a hack. In my code I could have a `TraceConfig` subclass which holds the context in `TaskLocals` and exposes the `async_contextmanager` to set it. 

For this reason my suggestion is to allow dynamic changes of `ClientSession._trace_configs` itself, without the need of changing calls to methods making requests.

Tornado automatically makes `json.dumps` + set content-type when it see that `isinstance(data, dict)`. This covers many usecases. But even with this, we needed to patch tornado. We send lists as json. So, as I think, most intuitive API will be:

* `post(json=..).` -- calls `json.dumps()` and sets content-type `application/json; charset=utf-8` (if content-type was not set using `headers=...`
* `post(text=..).` -- calls `.encode('utf-8')` and sets content-type `text/plain; charset=utf-8` (if content-type was not set using `headers=...`

This will match how the `web.Response()` works.

> How to manipulate cell metadata? I can see that some nbextensions can set metadata and the metadata could be saved in .ipynb, but I am not sure how the kernel can access cell meta information.

The kernel can't directly manipulate cell metadata: by design, it doesn't know about the document the code is part of. You can display Javascript output which will manipulate cell metadata, or even set up a comm to exchange data between the frontend and the kernel.

> How does user_expressions work? I mean, how the frontend determines which user_expressions to evaluate? The user_expressions looks to me the only way for the front and backend to exchange meta information, but I am not sure how to control the frontend.

I don't think the notebook uses `user_expressions`, and I'm not even sure that any of our frontends do. It was specified in the protocol to allow custom prompts in the frontend,  but we never got round to implementing that, and it hasn't proved very useful for anything else, as far as I know.

You might be best off looking into [comms](http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/comms.html) to allow custom messages between the frontend and the kernel.

It looks like tornado imposes a maximum size of 100MB for HTTP requests by default, and I don't think we currently override that anywhere:
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/a97ec9569b1995d8aa3da0a7f499510bffc006a3/tornado/iostream.py#L154

In the long run, the fix will be to maintain notebook models on the server, so we don't have to send the whole notebook over HTTP at once. But we should probably increase that limit as an interim measure.

As @lkraider pointed out in #355, manipulating a field's value in the midst of a validation run can be somewhat precarious. Also, I believe that cleaning up a field's value is mostly associated with the import stage. In contrast, validation may be performed multiple times during the lifetime of a model instance.

For these reasons, I'm now thinking validators should only be able to accept or reject the value.

Having the ability to automatically sanitize or normalize a field makes sense to me, but I think it should be an independent step, not a part of the validation routine.

If a value must be adjusted in response to some validation error, it's better that the validator communicates this information in the error object, and the modification is done outside the loop. Error objects are in need of an overhaul anyway, and it would seem like a good idea to make room for error codes and similar metadata.

If there was a way we could make a lot of assumptions about what our users want, or if there was a lot of redundant information in the `peru.yaml` file we could fill out, then it might make sense to support some kind of init ourselves. But we try to be pretty agnostic about the kinds of projects peru ends up in, and redundancies in `peru.yaml` are hopefully design mistakes that we can fix. So I'm going to close this one for now.

I've imagined a similar idea before, maybe some kind of global config like this:

```yaml
everything_goes_here: my/modules/dir/

git module foo:
  ...
```

That would save typing in the simple case where just want to fetch a bunch of different modules all to the same place. The big problem in my mind is that there doesn't seem to be a good way of having a feature like this interact with regular `imports`. Here are the options I can think of:

- Using `imports` disables the magical default paths. That would mean that as soon as your project gets more complicated, you can't use this feature anymore, so the feature wouldn't be very valuable. It would also mean that simple projects and slightly-more-complicated projects work very differently, which could be confusing.
- Using `imports` is in addition to the magical paths. I don't think anyone would put up with duplicated folders in their sync dir. This would end up working like above, with projects going all magic or nothing.
- Modules that show up in `imports` are automatically excluded from the magic. This might be the best option for saving keystrokes, but it makes things more confusing. Now the new field means "every module goes here unless it appears in this other place." And now `imports` means "here's everything you're going to get in your sync dir, unless you have this other setting turned on".

The problem at the end of the day is that we don't want to have two ways of defining imports that hate each other. We'd really prefer *one* feature that works well for both cases.

I could imagine something like allowing `*` globs on the left side of imports, so you could do something like "everything goes here" or "everything starting with `js-` goes here". An issue with that is now there's a difference between globbed imports (which have to tack on an implicit subdir name) and unglobbed imports (which are put literally where you said). And also I'm not excited about throwing `imports: {'*': ./}` at the top of hello-world.

The underlying C library, libpostal, is not thread-safe, so releasing the GIL and allowing threads will almost certainly have unintended consequences.

Libpostal is quite fast - as in process every address in OpenStreetMap in just over an hour on a single core fast. IMHO it's premature optimization to worry about multithreading unless there's a concrete use case where greater throughput is needed _within_ a single process (it's totally fine to use multiple shared-nothing processes, MapReduce, etc. on multicore machines).

Here some thoughts about the ``BasketView``:

I could move  [L247-L265](https://github.com/itbabu/django-oscar/blob/414ad1ccfaa33b4be2c84cc34ac5e0e9a18043ab/src/oscar/apps/basket/views.py#L247-L265) lines outside ``formset_valid()` in a proper method.
The  other signal related lines are simple send calls.

The tricky part is that signals are raised  only if specific conditions are met:

* A `save_for_later` event disregards any other change in the basket, is available to signed in users only, raises a removal signal for its line.

* The `remove` event saves any other change in the basket, is available to anyone, raises a removal signal for its line, raises addition/removal signals.

* The `update` event saves any other change in the basket, is available to anyone, raises  addition/removal signals.

Using form and formset for raising signals means:
* pass ``request`` and maybe others parameters to forms and formset
* Modify the view behaviour since when `save_for_later` is `True` `formset.save()` is not called.
* loop multiple times the formset ( I'm not 100% sure about this atm)

OK so I believe I found a solution to this issue.

It all comes down to running as root or running via sudo, the use of multiple package locations for pip installation (lib/lib64, site-packages/dist-packages, /usr vs /usr/local).

I came across this similar issue:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33661818/python-virtualenv-importerror-with-celery-and-billiard

It turns out the pip and virtualenv versions that ship with Amazon Linux, even in the latest AMI, are out of date and (possibly) have bugs in how lib64/dist-packages is handled for virtual environments.

Updating both programs via pip and then recreating the virtual environment seems to fix the issue when running the setup as root (not using sudo).  The solutions given in other threads and this one to run pip from the virtualenv folder to reinstall dependencies are fixing a symptom, not the cause.

Similar issue reported here:
https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/letsencrypt-auto-openssl-module-not-found-ec2/4356

* "dumb terminal": show every character, allow basic control codes (LF, CR, TAB, BS, ...)  (default)
* "show controls": show most control characters, except newline
* "show ASCII only": controls and >127 are converted
* "hexdump": well, hex dump everything
* "raw": take the byte as-is, send to stdout without any translation

Explanation for the Code: We trained a model with GaussianDropoutLayers and I wanted to clean the model for the conversion. That's the reason for the use of .pop() and the transfer of the layer weights.

Execution Code:
```
from keras.layers import LSTM, Bidirectional, TimeDistributed, Dense
from sklearn.externals import joblib
from keras.models import load_model, Sequential
from keras.utils.layer_utils import print_summary
import coremltools
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelBinarizer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

filepath = 'ElmoClass_Belly/EVO-1228_5'
output_name = 'elementalMovementTypes'
model_name = 'ElmoClassBelly'

lb = joblib.load('ElmoClass_Belly/EVO-789/{}.pkl'.format('binarizer_pickle_protocol_2'))

isinstance(lb, LabelBinarizer)

model = load_model('{}/{}.hdf5'.format(filepath, 'lstm'))

model.summary()

model.layers.pop(7)
model.layers.pop(4)
model.layers.pop(1)

model.summary()

number_of_units_per_layer = 32

new_model = Sequential()
new_model.add(LSTM(number_of_units_per_layer, return_sequences=True, input_shape=(None, 6)))
new_model.add(Bidirectional(LSTM(50, return_sequences=True)))
new_model.add(LSTM(number_of_units_per_layer, return_sequences=True))
new_model.add(Bidirectional(LSTM(50, return_sequences=True) ))
new_model.add(LSTM(number_of_units_per_layer, return_sequences=True))
new_model.add(TimeDistributed(Dense(23, activation='softmax')))

new_model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Weight Transfer
for old_model_layer, new_model_layer in zip(model.layers, new_model.layers):

    weights = old_model_layer.get_weights()
    new_model_layer.set_weights(weights)

# Weight Check

for primary_key in lb.classes_:
```

Turns out you get only approximately 2-fold increase in speed but 30-fold increase in size. That's not worth it. This was tested on a fast SSD, which is probably not much affected by random access patterns. But SSDs will soon be the norm, so it seems not worth the trouble to optimize for HDDs.

Anyway, it's implemented as `ds.freeze()` and seems to work ok, so I'll leave it in for now.

In the course of testing this, I found a performance bug in `ds.scan()`. It spent ~80% of the time slicing the graphs. But sliced graphs are typically useless in `scan()` anyway (since edges between windows are severed). I made an option `include_graphs` with default `False` and this sped up scanning by 4-5-fold.

I also found that using `int16` instead of `float32` speeds up scanning about 2-fold, and reduces file size 2-fold. That's not even a tradeoff, so we should use int16 as much as possible.

Copying my answer from #225: 

If your hybrid model performs worse than a pure CF model, the following may be helpful.
1. Make sure your features are [appropriately scaled](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feature_scaling).
2. If your features are continuous, consider [discretization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discretization_of_continuous_features).

4. Consider inverse-frequency weighting of your features.

Another option is to reproduce the pvl_singlediode.m Matlab API by switching our singlediode's output from a DataFrame a simple dictionary. The dictionary values would be scalars or arrays as determined by the numpy broadcasting rules.

Here's another try at the multiple function approach. We'd have the existing singlediode that works with either scalar or time series inputs, and these functions that handle IV curve stuff for scalar inputs.

I don't know if one approach is better than the other.

def singlediode_points_to_frame(pnt):

    """

    Converts a dict of scalars describing the 5 points of

    a single diode curve to a 5 row DataFrame with

    columns current and voltage.



    Useful for plotting.



    Parameters

    ----------

    pnt: dict

        Typically the output of the singlediode function.

        Must have keys i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.



    Returns

    -------

    fivepnts: DataFrame

        Columns labeled voltage, current.

        Rows labeled i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.

    """

    fivepnts = {'i_sc': (pnt['i_sc'], 0),

                'p_mp': (pnt['i_mp'], pnt['v_mp']),

                'i_x': (pnt['i_x'], 0.5*pnt['v_oc']),

                'i_xx': (pnt['i_xx'], 0.5*(pnt['v_oc']+pnt['v_mp'])),

                'v_oc': (0, pnt['v_oc'])}

    fivepnts = pd.DataFrame(fivepnts, index=['current', 'voltage']).T

    fivepnts = fivepnts.sort_values(by='voltage')



    return fivepnts





def singlediode_ivcurve(module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current,

                        resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth, v_oc=None, num=50):

    """

    Creates a single diode IV curve with uniformly spaced points.



    Parameters

    ----------

    See singlediode



    v_oc: None or float

        Will be calculated if None.

    num: int

        Number of IV curve samples.



    Returns

    -------

    ivcurve: DataFrame

        Columns labeled voltage, current.

        Rows labeled by point number.

    """



    if v_oc is None:

        v_oc = pvsystem.v_from_i(

            resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, 0, saturation_current, photocurrent)



    voltage = np.linspace(0, v_oc, num)

    current = pvsystem.i_from_v(

        resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, voltage, saturation_current, photocurrent)

    ivcurve = pd.DataFrame(np.array([voltage, current]).T, columns=['voltage', 'current'])



    return ivcurve





def singlediode_points_ivcurve(module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current,

                               resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth,

                               num=50):

    """

    Solves the single diode model for scalar inputs and returns an IV curve

    and the 5 IV curve characterization points.



    Parameters

    ----------

    see singlediode



    Returns

    -------

    fivepnts: DataFrame

        Columns labeled voltage, current.

        Rows labeled i_sc, p_mp, i_x, i_xx, v_oc.



    ivcurve: DataFrame

        Columns labeled voltage, current.

        Rows labeled by point number.

    """



    fivepnts = pvsystem.singlediode(

        module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

    fivepnts = singlediode_points_to_frame(fivepnts)



    ivcurve = singlediode_ivcurve(

        module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth,

        v_oc=fivepnts.ix['v_oc', 'voltage'])



    return fivepnts, ivcurve

However, I do think memoization of the deserialized value is important (even without considering performance). Without a consistent value between `__get__`/`__set__`, you'd break reference equality:
```
class Foo(ApiModel):
    guid = UuidField('guid')

foo = Foo()
x = uuid.uuid4()

foo.guid = x
assert x is foo.guid  # FAIL
```
Obviously a contrived example, but this could certainly bite and subtly break things in unexpected ways.

From a performance standpoint, I don't think it's actually a premature optimization -- there's a general expectation that accessing fields is inexpensive. As an example, deserializing timestamp values into `datetime.datetime` objects using something like `dateutil.parse` can be shockingly slow and add up if the field is read many times.

---

@wintersteiger This was a deliberate design decision on my part. This is designed to avoid "shooting yourself in the foot". Build systems in general shouldn't emit build tree files into the source tree because
that prevents the same source tree from being used by multiple used for multiple different build configurations.

The bigger concern here though is I didn't want the generated build files from the python build system screwing up the CMake build. `version.h` is one such file. If we allowed this file to exist the `version.h` file generated by the python build system would end being up in the CMake build, which is completely wrong. This isn't so bad for `version.h` but there are other more important files generated by the build system which would cause havoc.

I deliberately included **NO CONVENIENT WAY TO DISABLE** the behaviour you're seeing because allowing the user to continue is simply too dangerous. If "you know what you're doing"(tm) the CMake code can be modified to remove those checks.

The full error message should be something like...

```
  " F:/dev/z3-cruanes/src/util/version.h should not exist and is polluting the source tree."
  " It is likely that this file came from the Python build system which"
  " generates files inside the source tree. This is bad practice and the CMake"
  " build system is setup to make sure that the source tree is clean during"
  " its configure step. If you are using git you can remove all untracked files"
  " using ``git clean -fx``. Be careful when doing this. You should probably use"
  " this with ``-n`` first to check which file(s) would be removed."
)
```

As the code suggests you can run `git clean -fx` to remove the files generated by the Python build system.

Another way to do this is use the git's worktree feature.

```
git worktree add ../path branch_name
```

`../path` will be contain a the Z3 repo in a new branch named "branch_name".

Using git worktrees may be more trouble that its worth though.

Yes, when using the C API, you are responsible for reference counting. Every time you get a Z3_ast (or most other Z3 objects types), you wall have to call `inc_ref` and every time you remove a reference to a Z3_ast you have to call `dec_ref` by yourself. Like Nikolaj said, this is not necessary in the C++ API if you use `expr' objects, which do the reference counting for you, even when used in combination with pure C functions; see the C++ example to get started with that.

`Z3_solver_assert(...)` is the right function to add constraints to a solver, so they can be checked by  using `Z3_solver_check() `. See C++ example again.

```
>>> T.constant(1).dtype

'float16'
```

Actually, this is tricky because query strings are stringly-typed.

I think that `verify=False` in the URL *must* be interpreted as the string `'False'`, because it is impossible to distinguish between the boolean `False` and the filename `'False'`.

Interpreting `use_ssl` in the query string requires a mapping from string to booleans. Do we recognize `'true'` and `'True'` to mean boolean `True`? What about `'yes'` or `'1'? I am not aware of a well-defined convention that we can follow. And what do we do if the URL contains `'use_ssl=foobar'`?

Given these problems, I propose the following:

* require `use_ssl` in the opener URL to be one of the recognized values `{'true', 'True', 'false', 'False'}` and otherwise raise `ValueError` with an informative message
* always interpret the `verify` query parameter as a file path, except when it is empty, in which case interpret it as `False`

This feels kludgy. Suggestions for improvement?

While doing this I realized that most dependencies are packages as `.so` files inside the package, and end up in `/opt/nteract/`.

.deb archive linters will complain about using embedded libraries, as most correct way is to list those libraries as dependencies in the package metadata, so that the libraries can be installed from their own packages. Using `.so` files works fine, it's just not the preferred way.

I have a feeling that if it's  at all possible to avoid shipping libraries as `.so` using electron-build, it may be non-trivial (or at leat it's non-obvious to me). For the sake of this pull I'm not going to set this package to depend on other packages, and just let the libraries be shipped as `.so` inside of the package.

> Is it a right behavior?

That is the right behavior - I believe MRC and MCR are co-processor instructions, and VEX simply does not support them. angr will definitely stop decoding the block when there is anything undecodable.

> Or where I can patch this so that angr will not treat these instructions as the split of different blocks.

I believe now there is a way (a new and _advanced_ feature in PyVEX or gymrat) to write your own lifter, that explicitly lifts/patches byte sequences that VEX does not support otherwise. I think there is some documentation available, but I'm not sure where and how complete it is, and I really don't have time to look it up and post the link here right now. I would recommend you to take a look at [PyVEX](https://github.com/angr/pyvex) and [angr-platforms](https://github.com/angr/angr-platforms), where gymrat is implemented, and where we wrote lifters for new architectures in Python. Modeling the behavior of `MRC` or `MCR` is a different story, but at least you can nop them out ;)

The above is the correct way to solve this problem, but it might be take too much time to get started if you are new to angr, PyVEX, and angr-platforms. An alternative approach (which is a giant hack) is to manually patch the byte sequences of `MRC` and `MCR` in the binary to `nop` before lifting. You can do this using a hex editor and modify the binary, or you can modify the content in the memory backend (`proj.loader.main_object.mem` or something like that, I don't quite remember the full thing).

If you want to look at the contents of the file after it has been closed (which is the case here), then you will have to access it by its path and 
```python
print repr(found.state.posix.dump_file_by_path('_a\nb\tc_'))
```
The file's path was set in `license_name` in this example. 

You can see which files are left open after the analysis by looking at `found.state.posix.files` which will have a dictionary of file descriptors and their respective `SimFile`s

You can also see which files have been touched during analysis by looking at `found.state.posix.fs`.

If you have access to a SimFile `f`, you can also do `print found.state.se.any_str(f.all_bytes())`


NOTE: There is a subtlety when looking at the content of a file that is opened, modified, closed, opened, modified, etc. The respective SimFile in `posix.files` will have the most up to date content. The file's contents only get flushed back to `posix.fs` after it is closed. There are also some subtle things that can happen when opening the same file multiple times.

`SteamClient` uses `gevent`, so you don't need to use the threading module, in fact that will break things. Unless you use `gevent` to monkey patch it.

Keep your `machine` class and remove everything else. Then you can do

```python
vastrix = machine()

while True:
     vastrix.test()
     vastrix.client.sleep(5)
```

Keep in mind that if the network connection disconnect, you have not implemented anything reconnecting logic. I would look through the recipies in the repo, or docs for details on that.

The code you provided is not exactly easy to read. I suggest refactoring it into smaller pieces and generally making it readable.

Now I can't really answer you question, so let me tell you how `SteamClient` works. It uses `gevent` and every event is ran in a separate greenlet (think lightweight thread). In this case `end_match`. If there is any exception within that, then the greenlet dies. However,  the exception will not bubble up to your main thread and the application will continue to run. The exception will still print in your terminal. 

To clarify, you might have an exception somewhere in your code.

Also, keep in mind that if you do any IO blocking operations within your code it will yield to the gevent hub, which might schedule another greenlet that could change your state.  So when your `end_match` greenlet resumes, stuff might be missing, or the `SteamClient` might have reconnected, or who knows.

The problem you have is probably related to timestamps.
If the `ATT_POS_MOCAP` message has a timestamp "too old" relative to the autopilot's time, [the PX4 firmware detects a timeout](https://github.com/PX4/Firmware/blob/b5be990109a00814102005b6b809d64daadc40d8/src/modules/local_position_estimator/sensors/mocap.cpp#L117). If a timeout is detected, [the message is ignored](https://github.com/PX4/Firmware/blob/b5be990109a00814102005b6b809d64daadc40d8/src/modules/local_position_estimator/sensors/mocap.cpp#L59).

So, you *must* sync the Pixhawk's clock with the RPI's clock (e.g., by using the [MAVLink TIMESYNC messages](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/mavlink/vz3msJllpSU)) and make sure that the timestamps in the ATT_POS_MOCAP messages are "not too old" w.r.t. that clock.

>From my reading of the documentation, the default pipeline includes an in-memory cache, which I assume means that repeated calls to the same API won't be needed. But experimenting, I see the same API being called multiple times:

You're correct in reading the documentation that the in-memory cache prevents the same calls from needing to be executed more than once. There are a few exceptions, and matchlist/match history is one of them. The reason you're seeing the same URL hit multiple times is because Cass is using different parameters to access different parts of a summoner's match history each time. This is necessary since Riot changed matchlist to only return at most 100 games for each call the user makes. If you don't want the entire match history, simply stop iterating over it after 100 matches and you will only make one call to the Riot API.

>My reason for being concerned about API calls is that I'm not clear to what extent I can set up a persistent store in the pipeline and end up being able to run a data analysis program twice, the second time needing no access to the actual API and serving everything from the store.

You can absolutely set up a persistent store in the pipeline and only the pull the data from Riot once. That's exactly what it's meant for, and we have a simple disk-based database that I've been using that does this really well (and I can run data analysis programs while completely disconnected to the internet).  However, again there are a few exceptions to what data we store permanently. The match history of a summoner is one such example: It's non-trivial to know if a summoner's match history has been updated, and therefore when new data needs to be pulled. Therefore right now we don't cache or store match history results for a summoner, and every time a program needs to be run the match history needs to be pulled again. However, just about everything else is stored properly! Match history is the only exception I can think of.

There are some ways to properly cache or permanently store a summoner's match history, but it requires quite a bit of new logic. This is on our todo list, but not super high. We would need functionality to store partial-match-histories and then need logic for updating that match history whenever a summoner plays a new game.

>This may be needed anyway, to an extent, as I've not yet determined how the "recent matches" API would handle being called at a later time with a persistent store that's from a previous time - but even if I can't do this, I'd like to still just store match IDs and rely on the store to avoid API access for match data by ID.

I didn't exactly follow this, but it sounds like you might want the functionality to store partial match histories. In that case, storing the match IDs separately would be one solution (assuming you don't need the other data that comes with the match history), and you could use a separate database tightly coupled to the datapipeline to handle all your other data storage.

>BTW, is there a way to set my default region as an environment variable or similar?

No, there isn't a way to set the default region via an env var. You have to set it in your configuration/settings, set it programmatically like you did in your example, or specify the region for all objects individually.

So to follow up on this:

For the documentation, I don't have much guidance at this time. If someone has some proposals of what they'd like to see, I can provide some feedback.

As for ``nan`` vs exception, I think the behavior I'd most like is for an individual featurize() function (for one object) to throw an exception if something goes wrong. For example, if the featurizer requires cations and anions (needs + and - charged sites) but you feed in a metal where all the charges are zero - to throw a meaningful exception like RuntimeError("no cation site found"). Hopefully not just a random failure of some line in the code unless the user tries something really crazy (e.g., if they throw in a crystal structure to a composition object, a weird failure is fine).

Note that I am saying this because the ``featurize_dataframe()`` method includes an ``ignore_errors`` key, so I am assuming that if 999 of my entries are good and 1 entry is incompatible with a featurizer, I can still call featurize_dataframe and get data for the 999 and ``nan`` for the 1 without everything crashing. Subsequently, I can use pandas to fill ``nan`` values with 0 or some other value I like.

When `graphene_django` converts a field with `choices` to `graphene.Enum`, it creates a new type for this field.

If it was `type` field of model `Foo`, new autogenerated type will be `FooType`.

And you probably already have your own

```
class FooType(DjangoObjectType):
     class Meta:
         model = Foo
```

So there is `AssertionError: Found different types with the same name in the schema: FooType, FooType.`

This conversion raised a lot of questions already: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-django/issues?q=is%3Aissue+choices

Maybe someone adds `GRAPHENE_CONVERT_CHOICES_TO_ENUMS = False` setting to `graphene_django` once.

Until then I am going to monkey-patch it somewhere before schema is imported, for example in settings file.

Patch: https://gist.github.com/denis-ryzhkov/fcb944f6ebfad4775efb74848703f0d7

Automatically sending all `@property`s along would be a large assumption. We have a few cases where we want `@property` attributes to show up in the schema, and we have just been able to do:

```
class MyObject(DjangoObjectType):

    name_of_property_on_model = graphene.String()
    ...
```

And then it is getting picked up automatically and doesn't need any custom resolving. Of course, you should use whichever graphene type matches the property's return value. Let me know if that works for you too.

I'm wholly in support of this. We need the FileWriter to write the `scalar_pb`s or other endpoints, so TensorBoard can't really be used w/o the TF api until we export these symbols.

Another thing this gives us is much more flexibility for adding new features. E.g. I think we should add additional run-level metadata, like run descriptions and hyperparameters. To write this metadata, we will probably need to reify an explicit "Run" construct which might need a special run-aware FileWriter, or we might add some APIs to the FileWriter to support Runs. It's easier to do that if the APIs are sourced from our codebase rather than the TensorFlow codebase.

I think we probably should have Summary and Event be exposed by TB api as well, but while we're redesigning the backend, we might want to leave ourselves the option of deciding what to export for later, since it's easy to add stuff to the api and hard to take it out. (What if we wanted to define our own Summary and Event protos which extend or diverge from the TF ones? Would that even be feasible?)

> users still have to "know how to do build systems well"

Yes, but this isn't your problem. Users already have to know how to do build systems to the extent required to build the functional parts of their software, because their primary goal as software developers is to deliver those. hotdoc doesn't do anything to address that, and that's fine, *as long as it doesn't make matters worse*.

If srcdir!=builddir builds are broken, and someone has a reason to want them to work, then they'll eventually fix the srcdir!=builddir case and send a patch, because a failed srcdir!=builddir build of the functional parts of the software is really obvious: the software fails to compile, or fails to link, or fails to run (in other words, fails to achieve its primary purpose). Documentation is non-functional, and still "works" when incomplete: if srcdir!=builddir builds work but result in subtly broken documentation (bits missing or whatever), that's less noticeable, and likely won't get noticed or fixed for a long time. I share @pwithnall's concern here about duplicate file-lists inevitably going out of sync.

Of course, it's easy to say "the maintainer shouldn't have allowed the file lists to get out of sync" or even "hotdoc should have made their build fail because the file lists were out of sync", but this is documentation - a secondary deliverable alongside the functional aspects of the software, and one that many developers only do reluctantly. Anything that makes the developer do work that could have been avoided/automated is a potential reason to put off documenting the project, or fixing its existing documentation.

> it makes it slightly less easy to run a server from that configuration file, or instrument it in any other way, I kind of like the idea of having the hotdoc.json file self-contained

I like @pwithnall's plan of passing the files to be documented as arguments to hotdoc, particularly from a "don't repeat yourself" perspective. Make and Automake are primarily designed to run compilers which work like this, and if you squint at it from an appropriate angle, Hotdoc is a compiler too.

If you want a self-contained `hotdoc.json` *as well*, then I would suggest writing an augmented version of `hotdoc.json`, which *does* include detailed file lists formed from its command-line arguments, into hotdoc's output (in `$(builddir)`) - a lot like gtk-doc's Devhelp XML file, or Doxygen's XML index, or even hotdoc's own sqlite database. The parts that are meaningfully human-editable could either be more command-line arguments (as they would be for `gcc` or `g-ir-scanner` or `gtk-doc`), or a human-edited file in the same syntax but without the explicit file lists (a bit like Doxygen's Doxyfile).

The human-editable source code `hotdoc.json.in` is not self-contained *anyway*, because it has Automake substitutions which need to be substituted before it can be used; and in larger/more complex projects (the ones that really need good documentation), it's quite likely to be documenting generated source files (Automake `$(BUILT_SOURCES)`, etc.) which will need to be compiled before Hotdoc can offer reasonably complete documentation. Given that, there isn't a huge difference between having `./configure` generate the `hotdoc.json` that you want, and having `make` (indirectly invoking hotdoc) generate it?

> requiring that a source list be updated when a project using autotools makes use of hotdoc is not such a price to pay

You're the author of a documentation tool, so by definition you've put considerable effort into documentation, and hence your point of view is skewed towards believing that other people will put effort into their documentation. :-)

After more debugging I noticed that, just before the problem happens, RAIL_EVENT_RX_FIFO_OVERFLOW is triggered.

My first guess was that if a frame requires an ACK and RAIL_EVENT_RX_FIFO_OVERFLOW is triggered then, depending on how Rail is implemented, it's possible RAIL_EVENT_RX_ACK_TIMEOUT is never called and efr32RadioProcess thinks that transmission never finished. The only problem with this theory is that, in every test, the frame that gets stuck in transmission does not require an ACK.

Nevertheless, I removed `otEXPECT(sState == OT_RADIO_STATE_RECEIVE);` from processNextRxPacket function and added `otEXPECT(status != RAIL_STATUS_INVALID_STATE);` just after RAIL_GetRxPacketDetails() call. This made sure that the RX FIFO is always drained, even if the radio is struck in transmission.

A naive implementation is probably as follows:

```python
from distributed.serialize import register_serialization

def serialize(model):
    import torch, io
    bio = io.BytesIO()
    torch.save(obj, bio)
    header = {}
    frames = [bio.getvalue()]
    return header, frames

def deserialize(header, frames):
    import torch, io
    [frame] = frames
    bio = io.BytesIO(frame)
    return torch.load(bio)

from distributed.protocol.serialize import register_serialization
register_serialization('torch.Tensor', serialize, deserialize)
register_serialization('...', serialize, deserialize)
...
```

I agree, it is confusing.  

However, there is an obscure yet valid reason for why it is done related to shortcut parsing logic and how shortcuts get sorted.  Suppose you define two shortcuts where one is a substring of the other, such as the built-in shortcuts ``@`` and ``@@``.  If you are not exquisitely careful about how you parse and sort the shortcuts you will end up with non-deterministic behavior.

So the way we do the logic is, suppose you have two shortcuts *a* and **ab**.  If you aren't careful about how shortcuts are processed, you might end up parsing "ab" as "shortcut a with argument b", which probably isn't what you want.

In an effort to further simplify cmd2, I actually recommend removing the **default_to_shell** flag.

Due to recent additions to cmd2, a user is able to tab complete both shell commands and their paths by using either `shell <name>` or `!<name>`.

Setting **default_to_shell** to _True_, offers the user a savings of 1 character (!) and does not tab complete shell commands anyway.

Keeping an outdated flag around results in pull requests like this due to maintenance complexity, since developers have to be aware of yet another setting whenever a change is made.

@kiranmr, I suspect the cause of the problem is differences in the value of the `HOME` environment variable when you ran `certbot-auto` yourself vs. when it ran from cron. You can verify this by seeing if you have two installations of Certbot on your system. Assuming you didn't customize where Certbot is installed using environment variables, one should be in `${XDG_DATA_HOME:-~/.local/share}/letsencrypt` if you're logged in as the non-root user that you originally ran `certbot-auto` with and the other is probably in `/root/.local/share/letsencrypt`. If this isn't the case, `certbot-auto` may have tried to update from `cron` and failed to do so, but this is much less likely.

We'll definitely fix this, however, I'd also like to encourage you to stop using `certbot-auto` and switch to our PPA. Prebuilt packages offer a much better user experience and even come with a cron job so you don't have to set one up yourself. You can find instructions for how to do this [here](https://certbot.eff.org/#ubuntutrusty-other). 

To fix this for `certbot-auto` users though, the easiest fix is the one implemented in #4500. A fix with a slightly larger scope is to put the Certbot installation somewhere like `/opt` so it can be shared. The downsides of doing this is we'd need to add a lock to `certbot-auto` and any installed 3rd party plugins would be shared by all users rather than just an individual one. This latter would avoid problems where a 3rd party plugin is available when running as a non-root user, but isn't available when running as root from cron though. The fix with the largest scope is to kill `certbot-auto` entirely which we will certainly do in the future, but I'm not sure it's feasible right now due to it's popularity, constraints on time, and political issues.

Yep. We can do it either way, it's just easier due to Certbot's architecture to have them in the same lineage. For example, currently at most one lineage is created during a run and is then passed around through Certbot code. If we put them in separate lineages, we'd have to change this or have people run Certbot twice with some kind of flag/prompt to distinguish between people who want to replace the certificates for a domain and those who want to add additional ones. Certainly not an unsolvable problem, but I believe @pde's position was putting them in the same lineage could save us a lot of work.

Responding to earlier in this thread though, I think we may be able to kick this decision to a later date. Regardless of how support this long term, I suspect we'll want people to be able to request a lineage containing only an RSA or ECDSA certificate. If this is the case, we could support ECDSA certs in their own lineage for now and potentially offer dual lineages later. I'd definitely have to get more of the Certbot team on board with this decision though.

I did some research on this when deciding whether Sendmail support was sane or feasible at all. What I was able to determine was that Debian has a bunch of scripts that modify/expand the sendmail config (which is Turing complete, of course) to provide a sane, simple STARTTLS config.

You can see this when you install Sendmail on a debian system:

```
Creating/Updating SSL(for TLS) information
Creating /etc/mail/tls/starttls.m4...
Creating SSL certificates for sendmail.
Generating DSA parameters, 2048 bit long prime
This could take some time
.................................+...+........+.+.+....+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++*
..+..........+...+..+....+.........+...........+...................+.................................+.....+...............+......+..+....+......+............+.........+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++*
Generating RSA private key, 2048 bit long modulus
..........................................................................+++
.........................................................+++
e is 65537 (0x010001)

*** *** *** WARNING *** WARNING *** WARNING *** WARNING *** *** ***
Everything you need to support STARTTLS (encrypted mail transmission
and user authentication via certificates) is installed and configured
but is *NOT* being used.

To enable sendmail to use STARTTLS, you need to:
1) Add this line to /etc/mail/sendmail.mc and optionally
   to /etc/mail/submit.mc:
  include(`/etc/mail/tls/starttls.m4')dnl
2) Run sendmailconfig
3) Restart sendmail
```

These outputs are (if I hazily recall correctly) a postinst script. It would probably be best to include a (possibly customized) version of that script in our plugin, and run it to allow the comparatively friendly `/etc/mail/tls/starttls.m4` file:

```
cat /etc/mail/tls/starttls.m4
divert(-1)dnl
####################################################################
##### This file is automagically generated -- edit at your own risk
#####

#####		version: 8.15.2 2018-03-20 00:49:16 cowboy
##### by: pde@graphene
##### on: Tue May 22 15:23:16 2018
##### in: /
##### input files: /etc/mail/databases
#####
##### Usage:
#####	1) To get *ANY* STARTTLS support for sendmail you
#####		A) *MUST* Add this line to /etc/mail/sendmail.mc
#####		   `include(`/etc/mail/tls/starttls.m4')dnl'
#####		B) *MAY* Add the same line to /etc/mail/submit.mc
#####		   to get MSP<->MTA authentication/encryption
#####	2) You may modify the marked portions of this file, those
#####	   deal with the cert/key filenames and TLS options.
#####	   If you need finer control of TLS options, use the access
#####	   database.

####################################################################
divert(0)dnl
VERSIONID(`$Id: starttls.m4,v 8.15.2-11 2018-03-20 00:49:16 cowboy Exp $')
dnl #
dnl #---------------------------------------------------------------------
dnl # Bring in Autoconf results
dnl #---------------------------------------------------------------------
ifdef(`sm_version', `dnl',
`include(`/usr/share/sendmail/cf/debian/autoconf.m4')dnl')
```

#2208 is turning out to be a nightmare with us finding more and more problems that need to be solved, so I only had time to write down high level comments here before the holiday break. This is long and loosely formatted so I apologize and try to bear with me.

I think you should look over my explanation of why I chose this approach that I emailed you and if you think we should go with something else, let me know what you prefer and why. Given that we'll be living with this interface for a while, I think it's worth designing it as carefully as we can.

One of my main goals when adding to the plugin interfaces is to make it easy to implement as we can. I think the reason hooks exist is because writing Certbot plugins is too hard and requires too much code. I bring this up now because I think this motivation affects a number of my other comments here.

As for specific things in this PR, what do you think the benefit of `verify_installer_specific_updates` is? The main example you mentioned in your original post is `--disable-installer-updates`. I'm not sure if we should have this flag. My main purpose when adding this interface was to provide something that third party plugins could use to provide enhancements without blocking on changes landing in Certbot's core. Adding this flag introduces the problem you described and forces the plugins to write code checking if this flag and any of their installer specific updates flags were set. I also think the notion of an installer specific update is largely internal and not clear to most users.

The only other reason I could come up with for this method was to allow plugins to use something like `IDisplay` to ask questions about the enhancements selected early in execution. Perhaps this is valuable, however, a plugin may be able to do this during a function like `prepare()`. I really haven't thought about doing this much, but as this was the only other reason I could come up with. If there are things I am missing, let me know.

Also, I feel like with the approach here, every "updater" method we ever create will take a `lineage` parameter to signal whether or not the cert was renewed. This feels clunky. If certificate renewal is something we should signal to the plugin, perhaps we should do so generally outside of this interface. The way I'd do this is yet another simple abstract class that defines a method that is called on renewal. Using this approach, plugins that implement enhancements that want to do something special during renewal can do so and we don't have to duplicate this parameter across all of our updater methods.

As you can tell, I quite the fan of these simple ABCs. We can provide meaningful implementations of them in `certbot.plugins.common` and plugins can pick and choose the functionality they want/need and ignore (and not implement) those they don't.

A more minor point here, both you and Erica were confused by my class names and documentation here. If you like this general approach, but think this can be improved, please do so. I think we should have a common name like "Updater" to group these related classes together, but otherwise, I'm pretty open to the approach here.

Again, sorry for this ramble and let me know what you think!

On Thu, 21 Dec 2017, Ed Simmons wrote:

> mavnative/mavnative.c: In function 'py_mavlink_parse_char':
> mavnative/mavnative.c:155:5: warning: enumeration value 'MAVLINK_PARSE_STAT
> E_GOT_BAD_CRC1' not handled in switch [-Wswitch]
>      switch (status->parse_state)
>      ^~~~~~
> 
> It looks like this ought to be handled properly.

Welll... no, not really.

This is where we handle the case that the first checksum byte doesn't 
match:

https://github.com/ArduPilot/pymavlink/blob/master/mavnative/mavnative.c#L253

We check to see which state to go to based on the current byte.

If we were to add a state for MAVLINK_PARSE_STATE_GOT_BAD_CRC1 then we may 
unnecessarily drop an entire packet (because we'd be dropping the stx 
which was received out-of-place).  Or we'd have to do some sort of redo of 
the switch which would be nasty.

I think the best way forward here is to squelch the warning; drop a case 
down in which basically explained why the state isn't required, and throw 
whatever error we can if we do hit that state.

We can't remove the define as it is part of the API.

I don't think one problematic sample justifies a change of the default value here. After all precision can be easily changed if required for very specific edge cases. If you look at the path data it's apparent that the visual difference is a result of a very unfortunate mix of relative and absolute coordinates that every halfway decent SVG editor would have avoided.

I used Scour a lot and the default precision was always more than enough. After all we're talking about 5 significant digits! That's a lot and the error introduced should typically be tiny and mostly invisible even at larger sizes.

In this case the only question is if we can optimize the parsing of path data to avoid this problem altogether... If all processing is done with full precision and the number of significant digits is reduced as the last operation this should be avoidable.

Easier is a relative term but yes Ember (with ember-cli-crudities) will makes things more flexible mostly because it has a data layer to talk to api's (ember-data) the same way Django has an ORM. But the principle of datasource is indeed the same.

It might be a personal bias, I just have the feeling that Ember with its data layer can make things more flexible. Like leveraging relationships on the frontend.
Like if you want to create a form for an `Invoice`, the invoice has a `Party` (foreign key) and for that invoice you need an active billing address of the party. You can achieve it like that:
```
{
  "name": "address",
  "required": false,
  "widget": "foreignkey",
  "label": "Address",
  "extra": {
    "related_model": "crm/address",
    "property_path": "party.addresses",
    "query": {
      "is_active": true,
      "is_billing": true
    },
  },
}
```
Since Ember is aware of data relationships it knows about the foreign key between invoice and party as well as the one-to-many relationship between party and address.
You could of course achieve the same result with Alpaca just you would have to do it "outside" of your database structure (I'm not sure I'm being very clear here)

In the long-term, Ember makes it easier to keep a more structured code-base. You can can keep a jQuery code-base structured as well, it ust doesn't come as easily

A couple of notes:

- you can't blindly forward _any_ arguments to `super().__init__()`, ie you should pass only those accepted by the super class
- in your case, it should be enough implementing an `__init__()` that do **not** calls the (non existent) super class implementation, but simply sets whatever member on the instance itself:

```python
>>> import rapidjson
>>> class MyE(rapidjson.Encoder):
...   def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
...     print('__init__', args, kwargs)
...     self.foo = 'bar'
... 
>>> e = MyE(sort_keys=True)
__init__ () {'sort_keys': True}
>>> e.foo
'bar'
```

When using the python runner on cloud instances the configuration of memory and GPUs is fixed and the cloud instance type is under the control of the studioml yaml configuration file.  In these cases access to the GPU resources are exclusively yours.  Which leaves us with the case where you are running the studioml python runner on your own private hardware, such as your laptop etc.

In these cases the amount of memory consumed by other applications is not fixed and the runner would not be able to make assumptions about when external processes might start, or stop.  Which means that the only way to have partitioning of memory is either by processes interacting with each other to co-ordinate memory consumption, or to have the GPU driver do this for you and this is not a supported cuda feature.

I sent a private DM about doing this in a cooperative manner between applications.  In the meantime lets close this for now and reopen when we can maybe describe a future feature or strategy to address this type of requirement.  We can reopen the issue if you would like re-surface the discussion again.

@markotoplak 

> ...what do you mean with axes definitions?

For spectroscopy and hyperspectral imagery, we will need to define what the dimensional axes are. For spectroscopy, for example, we will have a spectral axis, such as "Wavenumber ", "nanometers", or "eV". If there are multiple spectra, then the 2nd axis will be "replicate number" or possibly a time value, e.g., "seconds". In hyperspectral imagery, we will need (e.g.) "x", "y", spectral axis, possibly "z" and "time" for 5-D.

So when I'm speaking of the axes, I'm referring to vectors, 1 for each axis. Alternatively, for equi-spaced systems, one could define start, stop, and step. For "x" this could be (0,10,0.1).

With HDF5 files, we have a lot of possibilities. One can have a dataset vector that defines an axis. Similarly, one can have a ["scales" property](http://docs.h5py.org/en/latest/high/dims.html) which a special property in which a dataset vector is linked to a particular axis of another dataset. Finally, we can get axis information from the attributes (metadata), which can be a vector or start-stop-step data.

My system uses metadata (dataset attributes) to calculate the vectors for x, y, and frequency, and it can handle metadata vectors alternatively. Scales, though, are also quite handy-- a feature I only learned about in the last year.

Example spatial metadata for my dataset (key : value):

```
Raster.Fast.Axis : X
Raster.Fast.Start : 1.0
Raster.Fast.Stop : 199.0
Raster.Fast.StepSize : 0.662207
Raster.Fast.Steps : 300
```

Example frequency calibration:
```
Calib.a_vec : np.array([-0.1677, 863.4479])  # This is polynomial fitting parameters. Can be expanded to any order
Calib.ctr_wl : 729.994  # Spectrometer center wavelength
Calib.ctr_wl0 : 729.994  # Spectrometer center wavelength when calibrated
Calib.n_pix : 1600  # Spectrometer pixels
Calib.units : nm  # Units
```

Clearly this can be complicated.
